coredata.dc:title: Sensing of amino acids: Critical role of nanomaterials for the efficient biomedical analysis

coredata.dc:description: Amino acids (AAs) are essential, important, and integral parts of the human body that play a vital role in health. As important components of body metabolism, AAs are used as biocompatible materials for a variety of applications, including drug delivery, testing, human health, nutrition, and imaging. Due to their vital biological role, quick and accurate monitoring is very important in terms of human health. The application of various nanomaterials is of great interest in the development of sensors due to their unique properties compared to the bulk state. This review focuses on using advanced nanomaterials for fabrication of sensors and biosensors for AAs for efficient biomedical analysis of various diseases. The determination of AAs using various recognition methods such as optical, electrochemical, electrochemiluminescence, photoelectrochemical, and piezoelectric, in which more attention is paid to the presence of advanced nanomaterials and various biological factors are reviewed. Importantly, the role of different biological elements such as DNA, aptamer, antibody, enzyme, peptide on the sensing performance of AAs was surveyed, which is necessary for the selective analytical approaches for bioanalysis. Finally, the research and technological gaps are highlighted. It is important to point out that, this is the first comprehensive review in research area of AA’s sensing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amino acids (AAs) are essential, important, and integral parts of the human body that play a vital role in health. As important components of body metabolism, AAs are used as biocompatible materials for a variety of applications, including drug delivery, testing, human health, nutrition, and imaging. Due to their vital biological role, quick and accurate monitoring is very important in terms of human health. The application of various nanomaterials is of great interest in the development of sensors due to their unique properties compared to the bulk state. This review focuses on using advanced nanomaterials for fabrication of sensors and biosensors for AAs for efficient biomedical analysis of various diseases. The determination of AAs using various recognition methods such as optical, electrochemical, electrochemiluminescence, photoelectrochemical, and piezoelectric, in which more attention is paid to the presence of advanced nanomaterials and various biological factors are reviewed. Importantly, the role of different biological elements such as DNA, aptamer, antibody, enzyme, peptide on the sensing performance of AAs was surveyed, which is necessary for the selective analytical approaches for bioanalysis. Finally, the research and technological gaps are highlighted. It is important to point out that, this is the first comprehensive review in research area of AA’s sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids (AAs) are the main component of metabolism, and they are a precursor to several neurotransmitters and hormones. The main constructing blocks of proteins are the precursors of numerous metabolites, which include primary, secondary, and nucleic acids. They play an essential function in cellular metabolism and nutrition [1]. There is a concern that the protein imbalance of AA could have a direct and severe impact on human health [2]. All of AAs have a common feature which is the presence of both carboxylic acid and amine functional groups in their chemical structure. In addition to glycine, all AAs contain asymmetric α carbon, from which isoforms L and D are derived [3]. There is 20 protein-producing AAs in the human body, most of which are present in micromolar concentrations in plasma samples [4]. Isoleucine, lysine, phenylalanine, histidine, leucine, threonine, valine, tryptophan, and methionine are nine essential AAs that should be included in a balanced diet [5]. Other AAs can be synthesized from precursor molecules by the organism [6]. Cysteine, arginine, tyrosine, glycine, proline and serine are essential AAs in times of illness and stress [7–9]. Otherwise, they are known as unnecessary AAs. Finally, unnecessary AAs that are synthesized in the body are glutamic acid, aspartic acid, asparagine, and alanine [10]. The presence or absence of certain AAs and change of their concentration in the body can cause some diseases [11]. In this regard we refer to the biological role of the absence or presence of too many AAs in the body's biological systems: associated with hair loss, alzheimer's, muscle paralysis, epilepsy, liver problems, anemia, reproductive disorders, some cancers, emotional disorders, nausea, anxiety, Parkinson’s, cardiovascular disease, growth retardation. Additionally, AAs play a role in nitrogen balance, helping to absorb calcium, fighting viruses, affecting memory and learning, and so on [12–26]. Determination of AAs is offirst-rate significancefor clinical, pharmaceutical, and biochemicalresearchfor the evaluation of nutrients in food products [27]. To date,variousanalyticalstrategies like liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [28,29], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [30], gas chromatography [31], spectrophotometry [32], chiral ligand exchange capillary electrophoresis [33], fluorimetry [34], thin layer chromatography [35], colorimetric [36], and ratiometric [37] have beenused tothe identification theAAs in real samples However, a few of these methods have relatively high sensitivity, low-cost, and repeatable results [38]. Also, most of them suffer from limitation like low sensitivity, instability, time-consuming diagnosis, need for specialized and trained operator, tedious process and standard sample preparation [39]. Therefore, researchers are trying to develop new methods/tools to be used in the identification of AAs that overcome the limitation of traditional methods and also have various advantages. So, there is high demand for innovative diagnostic and analytical technologies. Sensing and biosensing technology is an of the efficient methods in sensitive and selective detection of AAs that have been considered by researchers [40]. Sensors are sensitive, cheap, simple, accurate, and stable tools towards screening of AAs in real samples [41]. On-site and in-vivo detection of analytes by sensors with high selectivity and no need to an expert operator are another advantages of sensors [42]. (Bio)sensors are a type of sensors with a biological element in their structures and increasingly shines in order to monitor human health [43]. However, the development of sensors is important as a bright and active field in biomedical analysis. The main part of detecting analytes in the structure of biosensors is known as bio-receptor [44]. The bioreceptor must have high specificity and sensitivity to the target molecule [45]. They can be classified based on the type of bio-receptor used in biosensors structure such as nucleic acid [46], enzyme [47], aptamer [48], cell [49], and antibody [50]. In nucleic acid-based biosensors, the main diagnostic component is DNA and RNA. The basis of the function of this sensor is the high tendency of connection between two single-stranded DNA strands and the creation of double-stranded DNA based on hybridization strategy, which led to its specific function [51]. Enzyme-based sensors use an enzyme as a bio-receptor for detection of the target analytes [47]. Most of these types of biosensors are based on horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and the glucose-oxidase (GOD) enzymes [52–54]. Pure enzymes have advantages like fast performance, high selectivity, binding to the test object and suitable catalytic activity that increases sensitivity of biosensors for the monitoring of analyte [55,56]. It should be noted that enzymes suffer from some limitation like I) losing their activity after a short time. II) Reducing their activity upon fixing on the converter. III) High cost of separation and purification of enzymes.

Aptamers are short, single-stranded oligonucleotides, and biosensors containing aptamers are known as aptasensors [57]. Aptamers can bind to target molecules with high specificity and affinity and are very suitable for diagnostic applications [58]. One of their disadvantages is their high cost [59]. Cell-based sensors have a similar structure to enzyme based (bio)sensors, except that they are more stable and less expensive than enzymes [49]. However, it should be noted that the cells require special storage conditions and also have poor selectivity [60]. Finally, immunosensors, which involve the binding of antigen to antibody, are capable of instantaneous analysis of complex mixtures and provide high accuracy and sensitivity [61]. A variety of sensors can be used as suitable tools for AAs analysis, like medical [62], agricultural [63], pharmaceutical [64], food quality measurement [65], and biological process control [66]. Also, there is various types of biosensors based on detection method like electrochemical (EC) [67], optical [68] (fluorimetry, fluorescence, UV, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and fluorescence or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)), electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [69], photoelectrochemical (PEL) [70] and piezoelectric [71] which are investigated and discussed for the monitoring of AAs.

The impact of nanomaterials’ application on catalysts [72], energy conversion [73], biosensors [74,75], sensors [76], and medicine [77] have been proven. Nanomaterials with very interesting properties have been considered in recent years. Controlled shape and proportions, a large surface area, and distinct physical and chemical properties are among these characteristics [78]. In line with the unique properties of nanomaterials, their use increases the analytical quality of sensors. Researchers have used a range of sensors to detect AAs in recent years, and a large number of articles in this field confirms the importance of the subject [79]. They have also used nanomaterials in most studies to develop some of the highly sensitive sensors and biosensors for the biomedical analysis of AAs.

In this review, we will consider the role of sensors in the monitoring of AAs. In this regard, the role of various nanomaterials in the design of sensors/biosensors for the recognition of AAs were highlighted. Significant and unique advantages of nanotechnology can lead to the emergence of sensors with high accuracy and sensitivity. In this review, we will survey the role integrated of methods on efficient AAs recognition in real samples. In addition, a general conclusion will be made for each AAs. In this study, research and technological gaps, comparison of different types of sensors/biosensors and the role of advanced nano-materials on the identification of AAs will be investigated and discussed. For this purpose, this review categorized based on the type of AA and role of method (optical, EC, ECL, PEC and piezoelectric)/ nanomaterials/ technique (labelled and label-free) on the sensing of AAs. Due to their vital role, quick and accurate monitoring is very important in terms of human health. The application of various nanomaterials is of great interest in the development of sensors due to their unique properties compared to the bulk state. This review is based on the application of advanced nanomaterials in sensing and biosensing of AAs towards efficient biomedical analysis of various diseases. This review is based on the determination of AAs using various recognition methods like optical, electrochemical, electrochemiluminescence, photoelectrochemical and piezoelectric with more attention on the presence of advanced nanomaterials and various biological factors. Importantly, the role of different biological elements (DNA, aptamer, antibody, enzyme, peptide and so on) on the sensing performance of AAs is surveyed, which is necessary for the selective bioanalysis. Also, critical role of advanced substrate on the sensing of AAs were reviewed for the discovery of advantages and limitation of different used techniques. Finally, the research and technological gaps are highlighted. It is important to point out this is the first comprehensive review in research area of AA’s sensing.

Cysteine (Cys) is one in the essential AAs for the body and is related with many necessary biological processes like detoxification, metabolism, and macromolecule synthesis. High Cys levels are associated with cardiovascular disease and neurotoxicity, and Cys deficiency is associated with hair loss, liver damage, weakness, and skin lesions. To this end, researchers have developed a series of sensors that can sensitively and selectively detect the target (Cys) [80–83].

EC sensors are sensors that use an electrode as a probe when an analyte is present. Modern EC sensors use multiple characteristics to identify different parameters like chemical, physical or biological parameters. They offer the benefits of the sensitivity, simplicity, and precision of the tool and can be easily extended as a numerous detection platform [84]. To date various types of EC sensors and biosensors are developed for the identification of AAs. One of important type of biosensors is based on HRP-enzyme for the bioanalysis of AAs. The HRP enzyme, which occurs in the roots of horseradish, is widely used in biochemistry. It catalase the oxidization of various organic substrates using hydrogen peroxide. Additionally, it has been used in diagnostic sensors. Also, copper, zinc superoxide dismutase (SOD1) is one of the important components in living organisms that plays a role in antioxidant defense and catalyzes superoxide anion radical to hydrogen peroxide. In 2010, Dharmapandian et al., [85], developed a biosensor for the sensitive determination of Cys using electroanalysis strategy. In this approach, they developed this system based on the oxidase–peroxidase activities with SOD1. The EC response of this two-enzyme electrode was obtained due to bicarbonate-dependent peroxidase activity stimulated by SOD1 thiol oxidase activity. The engineered biosensor of Cys showed a concentration linear range of up to 500 µM and a limit of detection (LOD) = 10 µM. The intelligent development of this system with bio-receptor is quite clear, but the absence of NPs is not ineffective in obtaining the normal LOD. In another example, Cao et al [86] designed an EC sensor that introduced great efficacy in the determination of l-Cys. The SPCE was adjusted with substrate gold (Au)/CeO2 composite nanofibers (CNFs) to generate an efficient EC sensor. Different quantities of l-Cys were detected by this system using cyclic voltammetry (CV) with LOD of 10 nM. It also had a concentration range of up to 200 μM. The usage of Au and nanofibers together boosted conductivity while simultaneously increasing l-Cys oxidation, resulting in excellent l-Cys detection performance. For catalysts, sensors, and electronics, copper ferrite (CuFe2O4) has recently been used with properties such as high reactivity, moisture compatibility, easy separation, and environmental compatibility. Recently, Atacan and collegues [87] determined l-Cys using a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with CuFe2O4/rGO–Au nanocomposite. In the presence of various concentrations of l-Cys, the constructed sensor showed a linear range between 0.05 and 0.2 mM and a LOD of 0.383 μM. The usage of Au NPs inserted into the nanocomposite was preferred to improve electron transport. Graphene together with Au NPs are created unique nanocomposites.

Newly, polymer-modified electrodes have been considered as a transducer for creating more active sites, greater stability, and better reproducibility. Also, Ziyatdinova and colleagues [88] introduced a sensor to determine l-Cys. The sensor is based on a deposited poly (p-coumaric acid) on multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) modified GCE. The introduced sensor has a linear range of 7.5 to 1000 μM and LOD of 1.1 μM. The clever use of the above strategy of high stability and conductivity properties of carbon nanotubes has resulted in a sensitive sensor performance. Screen-printed Au electrode (SPAuE) has attracted the attention of researchers as an analytical system for measuring various analytes. Interestingly, Kurniawan et al., [89] created a SPAuE electrodeposition with copper in a similar investigation. The sensor was engineered under optimal conditions with a linear range of 1 to 400 μM and 400 to 1800 μM and LOD of 0.21 μM. Although the above discussed method is innovative, simple, and cost-effective design, but not using NPs on the structure of sensor can be considered as vacancy. The presence of NPs as a probe on the surface of the electrodes is very popular. Yao and coworkers [90], proposed a sensor for l-Cys by electrodepositing of silver NPs (AgNPs) on the surface of GCE modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Fig. 1 ). The AgNPs/GQDs/GCE achieved in the concentration range of 0.1 to 5 μM and 8 to 200 μM showed a LOD of 0.01 μM. Nanomaterials developed from carbon are interesting. Graphene alone, despite having excellent properties, may be problematic due to its hydrophobic nature in aquatic environments. But compared to graphene, GQDs have some excellent properties such as low toxicity, chemical inertia, and optical stability, which lead to its preference in the development of sensors. It is important to point out that, the presence of AgNPs along with GQDs increases the electrical properties of the platform.

In a new approach, Wang and colleagues [91] synthesized and prepared graphene oxide/carboxylated MWCNTs /manganese dioxide/Au NPs (GO/CCNTs/AuNPs@MnO2) nanocomposites. The GCE was then modified with GO/CCNTs/AuNPs@MnO2. In comparison to l -Cys, the above system has a linear range of 0.01 to 7 μM and a LOD of 3.4 nM. This architecture can combine the properties of these materials including good electrocatalytic ability of MnO2, and unique properties of AuNPs such as good electrical properties, high surface-to-volume ratio, excellent surface properties, and small size. Additionally, combination of GO with CCNTs prevent the aggregation of GO plats and also create a remarkable electrical conductivity and electron transfer. The result of this combination is the design of a sensor that has fully utilized the properties of NPs in its development.

Different types of electrochemical sensors of l-Cys and theire analytical performance are summarized in Table 1 . Among the reviewed examples, Wang's system has performed better than the others in terms of LOD. By examining the EC methods collected in Table 1, it can be seen that NPs have been used in most of the researches. In this regard, GO/CCNTs/AuNPs@MnO2 nanocomposite has shown the best analytical result. Considering this issue, it is suggested to use the presence of new NPs such as dendritic fibrous nano-silica (DFNS) and metal–organic framework (MOF) nanocrystals in the development of new sensors for different analytes, especially for Cys in the near future. Unfortunately, only one research has used biological agents in its structure. Therefore, special attention should be paid to this case, which we recommend that more researchers use the excellent properties of aptamers, enzymes, peptides, and antibodies in the sensitive and specific detection of cysteine for the development of biosensors.

In another research work, Xavier and others [96], developed a fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon nano-dots (NCNDs) manufactured from nitrogen and carbon sources. The above sources are based on human urine residue and citrus extract. How resources are supplied has made this method considered cost-effective and a green method. The OFF-ON fluorescence method was used as a measurement of the target. It is noteworthy that the initial fluorescence NCNDs were turned off by adding Hg2+. The addition of Cys causes a competitive binding to Hg2+, and the fluorescence sensor switches from OFF to ON. The above method demonstrated a broad linear range between 1 and 10 μM and a LOD of 40 nM. NCNDs with physico-chemical properties like high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, low cost, biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization can be recognized as effective representatives of nanomaterials.

In 2021, Lu et al., [97] designed a fluorescence/UV/Vis optical sensor to determine l-Cys. In this approach, CDs were first synthesized and used for the sensitive determination of Cys, and it was found that this probe exhibited a LOD of 0.02 μM in the concentration range of 0.045 to 2.03 μM. The acceptable and sensitive performance of this method is achieved due to the presence of CDs. Also, Li et al [98] developed a new dual-signaling probe consisting of a typical aggregation induced publication fluorogen of 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)phenyl 2,4-dinitrobenzenesulfonate (TPE-NP). By using two fluorescence and UV/Vis absorption channels, Cys can be easily determined by this probe on various types of biofluids. The engineered UV/Vis-based probe successfully left a LOD of 0.21 µM for Cys in the concentration range of 20 to 400 µM.

When comparing the methods under investigation, it is clear that the second method has the lowest LOD among the other methods. Among the examined optical researches, the use of QDs has been of interest, but it should be noted that other NPs have not been used much. Therefore, there is a need to use other NPs (CdSe, titanium dioxide and pure zinc oxide nanostructures) that have significant optical properties to develop new optical probes. Also, the absence of biological agents in this part is clear, which is considered a vacuum. In addition, it is suggested to develop portable and low-cost optical sensors for the detection of Cys that are able to use and in situ determining of analyte.

The SPR method is one of the optical methods for calculating the refractive index of very thin layers that are absorbed on a metal surface. Under specific circumstances, surface plasmons on a metal layer may be excited by photons, and the result of this excitation is the transformation of a photon into a surface plasmon. SPR-based optical sensors can determine the surface of the chip, which is covered with a metal coating, without any labeling [99,100]. Diosmin is a known glycosylated polyphenolic compound that is mostly found in vegetables and fruits. Activation of Ag NPs with this compound through green synthesis is important for applications such as detection of AAs. Rithesh Raj et al [101] synthesized AgNPs capped with diosmin using green method and used for the optical sensing of Cys using SPR-based fiber optic which LOD is 0.0077 μM. Also, this approach had a linear range of 0 to 100 μM.

It should be noticed that, AgNPs-based optical sensors have been considered for their optical stability, adjustable optical properties, high electrical properties, as well as ease of operation with organic ligands. The basis of the performance of biosensors that contain DNA in their structures is based on the high tendency of the connection (hybridization) between two single strands to each other. This property leads to the specific function of the sensors that utilize these nucleic acids.

In another research work, Chang's colleagues [102] used a SPR based optical sensor based on the alteration of the structure induced by silver ions (Ag+) in a single-stranded cytosine-rich DNA to determine the Cys. With the addition of Cys, the formation of the C-Ag+-C assembly is inhibited because Cys is related to Ag+ in a competitive manner. Single-stranded oligonucleotide formation results from this competition. Increasing the signal increases the sensitivity, in which daunorubicin and DNA intercalator has been used. The measurement linear range was about 50 to 2000 nM, with a LOD of 50 nM. The above method consists of a simple target detection system. Ahmed and coworkers have recently reported a SPR based sensor for detection of l-Cys [103]. In this work Cys in the presence of Cr3+ was measured using SPR-based Au nano-probes (as a binder). An intense color variation from red to violet and colorless has been achieved by adding Cys to Au NPs in just one minute. The optical sensor had a LOD of 0.012 nM. The investigated concentration range is reported from 0.1 nM to 0.1 mM. Presence of Au NPs increases the diagnostic device's sensitivity. SPR-based Au nano-sensor was able to determine the amount of analyte in a variety of samples such as blood, human urine, and milk. For the three examples provided, the optimized system had a lower LOD and superior performance to the other two methods. In this section, it should be mentioned that in the development of optical sensors based on SPR, no biological agent was used for the development of the sensor. This can be considered as a shortcoming; however, biotechnology can be used in these types of systems to creating specific functionality in sensors which is one of the important properties of bioreceptors (especially aptamers).

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is an acutely strong luminescent technique that produces EC reactions in solution phase. ECL is a method derived from the combination of chemiluminescence and EL quantitative techniques [104]. Over the last few years, ECL has been developed as a new type of analysis method to identify different types of AAs. Properties such as simple performance, high sensitivity, extensive dynamic range, and low background signal have attracted the attention of this analytical tool [69]. Earlier, in section 2.1.2.2, the effect and properties of nucleic acid on sensing of l -Cys were mentioned. Interestingly, Tang et al., [105], successfully designed a biosensor for the sensitive detection of Cys. In this regard, they used a functional nucleic acid. This sensor had a linear range of 100 pM to 50 nM with a LOD of 50 pM. The intelligent presence of nucleic acid in the above approach has caused a very sensitive and accurate detection of the target molecule.

In 2018, Wu et al., [106], constructed a sensitive ECL device based on a dissolved oxygen system, platinum NPs (PtNPs), and rhombus-shaped rubrene micro sheets (RubRMs). Using RubRMs and PtNPs, they combined high catalytic efficiencies as well as electron transfer capabilities, which eventually led to more successful detection of l-Cys. In optimum conditions, a LOD of 0.33 nM and a linear range of 1 nM to 0.5 mM were obtained. PtNPs have been effective in this approach and have acted as signal amplifiers.

In one approach developed by Wu and coworkers [107], manganese dioxide carboxylated graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposite (MnO2/C-g-C3N4) was used as a probe in the on/off ECL assay to detect l -Cys in real samples (Fig. 3 ). Due to ECL-energy transfer from C-g-C3N4 to MnO2, C-g-C3N4 (nanosheet) and MnO2 (nanosheet) were used as donor and acceptor, respectively, which ECL of C-g-C3N4 can be quenched by MnO2. The addition of the Cys reduced MnO2 to Mn2+ and led to the removal of ECL-ET and, finally, the restoration of ECL intensity. The extended ECL sensing method confirmed a broad analytical range from 0.5 nM to 1 μM as well as a LOD of 0.18 nM. The presence of nanocomposite in this strategy led to a better response to the above method.

Recently, various polymers are used successfully in ECL based sensors as receptors. Polypyrrole is a polymer with good conductivity and stability as well as easy synthesis. For example, Karimi and colleagues [108] designed a chemosensor based o depositing layers of a hybrid nanocomposite of polypyrrole-dodecyl benzene sulfate-sodium perchlorate-sodium carbonate-gadolinium (PPy-Gd2O3)-based on platinum substrate. In this report, linear range was from 0.1 pM to 1 μM and with LOD of 42 fM for l-Cys. Gd2O3 as a metal nanoparticle with electron transfer and catalytic properties facilitates the increase of the ECL signal.

In summary, studies show that the LOD of this method has better accuracy and response than the target. In the investigated ECL approaches, the use of PtNPs, MnO2/C-g-C3N4 nanoplates and PPy-Gd2O3 nanocomposites can be seen, which shows the high importance of nanomaterials. Therefore, the use of other nanomaterials in the development of this type of sensor is recommended. On the other hand, only one study has used oligonucleotide, which suggests that enzyme, aptamer, and antibody should be used in the development of ECL biosensors to achieve better analytical results.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing is a highly sensitive method for the recognition of small molecules that is fast and easy to use [109]. The PEC method is a technique that has just emerged but is developing dynamically. Current and light serve respectively as signals and sources of excitation in the PEC detection process. The photoelectric conversion efficiency is one of the most critical characteristics that affect the detection sensitivity of PEC sensors. The photoelectric conversion efficiency of the sensor is determined by the photoactive substance stabilized on the electrode [70]. In 2017, Zhu et al., [110], thanks to a simple single-stage synthesis process (FSP), prepared the CuO-Cu2O for use in the FEC sensor. CuO-Cu2O was employed as the PEC for the detection of the analyte, taking into account the target's interaction with Cu-containing compounds via the creation of a Cu-S bond (l-Cys). Reduced CuO-Cu2O photocurrent response to addition this sensing system was noted. According to this report, the optical peak currents of the prepared sensor decreased from 0.2 to 10 μM after increasing the l-Cys concentration. It also had a LOD of 50 nM. Due to the great characteristics of this design like effective separation of the electron pair of the hole and strong absorption in the visible region, the nanocomposites on the response surface showed a good cathodic light current.

Interestingly, Shen and coworkers [111] synthesized reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites of (rGO)/CdS:Mn in an aqueous solution using a straightforward single-step technique. They developed a Cys-sensitive PEC sensor based on the rGO/CdS:Mn that had a linear range of 0.1 to 20 nM and a LOD of 20 nM for target analyte. Doped Mn ions can extend the duration of electron-hole pairs, trap electrons, and boost visible light absorption intensity. The addition of graphene in this nanocomposite boosted the electron-transport property, which boosts the light intensity and makes it useful for PEC.

Alos, Sun and coworkers [112], developed a PEC system using protoporphyrin IX (PPIX), WO3, and rGO chemical.Aqueous Cys was detected using this sensor stabilized on ITO electrode. The above-prepared sensor exhibited a linear range of 0.1 to 100 μM and a LOD of 25 nM. The rGO layer provided an electron transfer conductor bridge for the ITO electrode to anchor firmly with WO3 nanostructures. Among the PEL systems discussed, the group of Shen is more efficient due to its high accuracy. It was a simple technique that the presence of rGO NPs gave better performance. Also, the absence of biological agents is quite clear. It is recommended to design portable and cheap diagnostic chips for the use of most people in the future for better efficiency of this type of sensors using different types of bio-receptors (antibodies, enzymes, genes and DNA).

All of the reviewed articles for the sensing and biosensing of l-Cys are summarized in Table 1. As can be seen, the presence of various NPs (MWNT, AgNPs, GQDs, GNR, BCNT, H-BDDP, NPG, NCNDs, PtNPs, Gd2O3, WO3, Au/CeO2 CNFs, MnO2/C-g-C3N4, CuFe2O4/rGO-Au and GO/CCNTs/AuNPs@MnO2 nanocomposite) is confirmed in most of the existing studies. Referring to this issue, the great importance of NPs /nanomaterials is clearly revealed. It is worth noting that NPs increase the accuracy, specificity and sensitivity of analytes detection. Therefore, it is expected that metal nanoparticles, silicate nanoparticles, new MOFs nanostructures and new nanocomposites will be used in future research. On the other hand, the influence of biological elements (bioreceptors) is important in the specific and sensitive determination of the analyte. But, in few studies, they have been used, so this issue can be raised as a gap. On the selective detection of Cys by developed methods among the reviewed examples, the number of EC studies was found to be more than other methods, which indicates the superiority of EC studies over other methods. However, other analytical methods have been successful in the sensitive detection of Cys. To prove this, when we look at the obtained analytical results, its obtained that the best analytical data is found from an ECL method. It should be mentioned that relatively fewer data are available for PEL and optical methods, which hopefully more researches can be done done on these subjects in near future. In total, the reported studies for EC, fluorescence/UV/vis, SPR, ECL and PEC sensors had detection ranges of 0.0034 to 100 μM, 0.0013 to 0.21 μM, 0.000012 to 0.05 μM, 0.00000042 to 0.00033 μM and 0.02 to 0.05 μM, respectively. Moreover, there is no report for the biosensing of l-Cys using bioreceptors like aptamer and monoclonal antibodies. Thus, biotechnologist suggested that work in this area of research towards preparing novel aptamer for specific surveillance in real samples. Finally, it is expected that portable and accessible sensors will be produced for fast, low-cost and sensitive on-site detection of Cys, so that all people can know their health status without the need of medical centers.

Methionine (Met) is a key AA which plays a critical role in the body [120]. It is also the principal source of methyl groups for the formation of DNA and other molecules. Met is one of the sources of sulfur [121]. Elevated lecithin levels help to reduce the cholesterol levels in the liver and prevent fatty liver, skin, and hair disorders [122]. In addition, Met deficiency can lead to many diseases that affect human health, such as muscle paralysis, depression, hair loss, stunted growth, HIV/AIDS, and liver failure [123]. Therefore, reliable methods for determining of Met are in high demand. In this part of review, the role of nanomaterials on the performance of EC, optical, ECL, and PEC of sensor were investigated. Also, advantages and limitations of AAs’ sensing methods on the term of analytical approaches were reviewed.

Polylactic acid is a biodegradable and bioactive aliphatic polyester material obtained from renewable sources such as corn starch. This bioactive polymer can be present in sensors for sensitive determination of analytes. In a new approach, Kalinke et al., [124], architected a chemosensor to determine l-Met in real samples. They tried 3D printing electrode because it is simple and cheap. The surface of the electrodes was engineered with polylactic acid/graphene (PLA-G). Using this sensor, a linear range of 5 to 230 μM and a LOD of 1.39 μM were obtained. Also, the proposed sensor was able to successfully detect the target in blood serum. By exposing the electrode to graphene layers, electron transfer was performed better.

Ebrahimi and colleagues [125], used a CuO nanoflowers (CuO NFs)-modified GCE toward sensitive determination of Met. Looking at the techniques mentioned, it was found that CuO NFs/GCE has good electrode catalytic activity against target oxidation. The sensor presented a linear analytical response of 1 to 300 μM with a LOD of 300 nM. The presence of nanomaterials with good properties persuades researchers to use them. Providing a larger catalytic surface and increasing electron transfer are also properties of NPs. Therefore, researchers are trying to use NPs to improve the properties of different sensors. In the meantime, CuO NFs are no exception to this rule.

In 2018, Sasikumar coworkers [126] developed a suitable system to determine l-Met. They modified the screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) with Mn2O3. The prepared electrode registered a concentration range of 1 μM to 0.61 mM and also a LOD of 1 nM. This system has been used to determine the target molecule in buffer solution and food (marine) samples. In this system, Mn2O3 applications have been used in EC capacitors and diagnostic tools, but the absence of NPs is noticeable. In a study done by Jeevagan et al., [123], 1,8,15,22 tetraaminophthalocyanato-copper(II) (p-4α-CuIITAPc)-modified GCE was utilized for the determination of Met by DPV technique which linear range of this sensor was 50 to 500 μM with LOD of 27 nM. To achieve a susceptible answer, multiple materials can be used, like varied carbon-based nanomaterials, silica NPs, as well as metal NPs. Among the above four methods, although CuO NFs were used in Ebrahimi group's method to develop the sensor, the LOD of the third system is lower.

A series of fluorescence probes have been designed to detect different analytes. Among them, tryptophan is prominent because of its good fluorescence property. In addition, the indole ring of tryptophan has attracted attention due to the structural and functional importance of interactions in biology and chemistry. In 2013, a simple sensor based on signal-off fluorescence system was developed by Wang's and co-workers [127] toward monitoring of Met in real samples. In a buffer environment, Na2CO3-NaHCO3 and Cu2+ are capable of responding to tryptophan and forming a complex. Note that the complex has a strong electrostatic interaction which leads to the quenching of tryptophan fluorescence. Because of the strong affinity between calcium and thiols, it is recovered through the addition of Met fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity recovered with the concentration of Met under optimum conditions ranging between 4.8 and 30 μM with LOD of 1.4 μM. The presence of a biological agent in this sensing system somehow causes sensitivity in the performance of optical recognition regime.

Also, Nsanzamahoro and colleagues [128] provided an “OFF-ON” approach for sensitive and specific recognition of l -Met utilizing Si NPs as a fluorescent probe. The Si NPs exhibiting orange fluorescence with a quantum yield of 2.23 %. This optical probe was made in one simple step using 2–aminophenol and 3(2-aminoethylamino) propyl (dimethoxymethylsilane) (AEAPDMMS) as precursors and hydrothermal treatment. According to this report, the LOD of 0.07 μM was achieved in the concentration range of 0.1 to 45 μM. Fluorescent silicon NPs (Si NPs) are very appealing because they are devoid of heavy ions. According to literature most of Si NPs emit blue or green light, but orange or red-emitting of Si NPs are necessary for various applications.

Lin and coworkers [129] have designed a fluorescent method for the detection of l-Met. They employed a chemosensor based on the cationic pillar (5) arene (AWP5) in water. The procedure was that interfering agent (various AAs) which was added to the AWP5 solution, but only the target was identified. The LOD of this method was 0.546 μM. The derivatives of pillararene have been taken into account for their host–guest properties and good solubility in aqueous solution. These characteristics led to the invention of a sensor capable of detecting a target in water.

In another similar example, Yang and colleagues developed a fluorescence sensor based on the pillar (5) arene (SP5) [130]. For sensitive and selective detection of Met, they introduced the concept of two-site collaborative recognition method. As part of this, they developed a fluorescent based sensor with an ester functionalized pillar (5) arene. It demonstrates that aggregation-induced emission (AIE) fluorescence in DMF/H2O binary solution (7:3, v/v) was capable of detecting l-Met with a LOD of 28.4 nM. The aforementioned system was very sensitive; moreover, the interference factors in the identification did not cause any significant interference. At the same time, the findings showed that the ester functionalized pillar (5) arene fluorescent sensor was able to dual-site recognize l-Met via numerous supramolecular interactions. Based on this report, it’s obvious that the CH···π, H-bond interactions between l-Met and ester functionalized pillar (5) arene led to construction of fluorescent sensor. The Yang group sensor introduced a new method and a special idea for sensing, and it was also more effective and responsive. Of course, the above method requires extra development due to its novelty. Less use of numerous nanomaterials can be felt in these types of sensors. In order to achieve a more sensitive response, nanotechnology can be used in these types of sensors. Also, there is no report for the biosensing of l-Met using bioreceptors like DNA, aptamer, monoclonal antibodies and enzyme. Therefore, it is expected that many researches will be developed in this area in the near future.

MoS2, a typical semiconductor of transition metal di-chalcogenides, is generating an increasing amount of research interest. Li et al., [131], recently designed a PEC sensor using MoS2, a common metal-mediated disulfide. It was a signal-ON system that switched the PEC response by MoS2 to OFF after adding l-Met. The logarithm value of Met concentration was proportional to the reduction of the optical current signal in the range of 0.1 nM to 1 μM with a LOD of 0.03 nM, which had excellent selectivity. However, only one type of PEC sensor is registered to detect the analyte (Met). So, there is still good opportunities for the development of these types of sensors.

Aspartic acid (Asp), among many AA, is linked to a range of actions in living cells and contributes to the diagnosis of myocardial function. It also has a colorful role in mammalian metabolism. Asp is an important nerve exciter of the central neural system. Abnormal level of Asp in the body can cause problems such as epilepsy, liver problem, and stroke. Additionally, Asp is a precursor for glycine and glutamic acid. Therefore, computable analysis of Asp is critical for the primary detection of serious diseases that may challenge human life [132–134]. So, in this section various types of sensors for the identification of Asp were surveyed.

To date, there is some of sensing systems for the recognition of d-Asp and l-Asp in real samples. For example, Wei and colleagues [135] developed an EC sensor for effective, simple, and enantioselective discrimination of Asp enantiomers (D/l-Asp) based on (bovine serum albumin) BSA-coated titanium dioxide)BSA/TiO2). The EC interface between BSA and TiO2 was effectively built to enable a large number of chiral recognition sites. In the linear range of 1 to 1000 nM, enantioselective discrimination of D/l-Asp was realized on the interface with a strong linear relationship against the difference of impedance, with LOD of 0.37 nM for l -Asp and 0.94 nM for d-Asp. In this system, TiO2 provide enough surface area for BSA loading by utilizing the structural qualities of a big cavity.

In another study, Prasad et al. [136] successfully engineered a sensitive approach for the recognition of Asp. In this design, a new molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) matrix along with titanium dioxide nanoparticle/multiwalled carbon nanotubes (the whole layer is called MMN) was used for the modification of PGE to detect Asp enantiomers in real and aqueous samples. Increased electrocatalytic activity to respond to the oxidative peak current of Asp, suitable reproducibility, high stability and uniformity of the nanocomposite are the advantages of this sensor. The above system showed a linear response of 9.98 to 532.72 ng mL−1 and a LOD of 1.79 ng.mL −1 for l -Asp. The extraordinary combination of NPs and creation of uniform nanocomposite in this approach has caused good sensitivity. In 2019, Chen and coworkers [137] proposed a sensor for the sensitive detection of Asp. Using GCE modified with molecularly imprinted sol–gel (MIS) layers was obtained by combining molecular imprinting and sol–gel technology. The EC responses of electrochemically passive Asp, the triplex derivative of l-Asp, Cu2+(N-carbobenzoxy (C)-l-Asp), was used as the pattern for making of MIS. The obtained sensor quantitatively provided a LOD of 1.77 μM in the concentration range of 0.01 to 0.2 mM of l -Asp. In this approach, the absence of NPs has not led to very suitable analysis results. The simultaneous presence of the features of molecular imprinting technology makes this system to work efficiently.

In 2016, Prasad and colleagues [138] developed a printed enantiomer pair of polymer thin layers with integrated MWCNTs, which was inspired by the surface pencil graphite electrode (PGE) is able for selective discrimination of to examine the selective analysis of both Asp isomers (D/L). Approved system was able for selective detection of l-Asp and d-Asp with sensitivities of 1.17 and 1.16 ng mL−1, respectively. In this work, PGE was immersed in a solution of AuNPs at room temperature. The PGE was then spin-coated with a pre-polymerization mixture consisting of ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (as crosslinker), L/d-Asp (as templates), N-acryloyl-pyrrolidine-2,5-dione (as monomer) and α,ά-azoisobutyronitrile (an initiator). Application of constructed stamen for the determination of l-Asp was evaluated in the presence of ferricyanide/potassium solution because the isomers of this AA (d-Asp) was found to be inactive.

In another similar example, Prasad and Pandey [139] used a nano-layer (PI3AA (poly (indole-3-acetic acid)) electropolymerized in the existence of l-Asp) to develop a nano-sensor by a mechanism of doping/de-doping. Complementary binding sites have been developed by over-oxidation of Asp-PI3AA adduct thin layer covered on the modified PGE surface of the MWCNTs. The extended sensor (MI-PI3AA/MWCNTs-PGE) provided a LOD of 16 nM for d -Asp and 25 nM for l-Asp.

For analytical purposes, CNTs are considered for their special electronic properties and therefore for increasing the kinetics of reaction. However, the toxicity of carbon nanotubes is one of the main limitations of these nanomaterials. Nanoscale materials have provided a toolbox that did not exist before. Also, AuNPs increase sensitivity of sensors/biosensors by increasing electron transfer. In terms of analysis, the method of Wei's group was more efficient than the rest of the studies. Also, there is no report for the EC biosensing of Asp which used to be attention. It is suggested to use nanomaterials and bio-receptors for better productivity in the development of EC sensors for the determination of Asp.

Glucosamine is a food supplement that has been used in recent years to treat arthritis and can be effective in treating hepatitis, stomach ulcers, and rheumatoid. In 2014, Zhao et al., [140] designed a fluorescence sensor for Asp detection. To achieve this system, they modified the near-infrared cyanine probe with glucosamine. In this approach, with the addition of Asp, a color change from pale red to colorless was visible. The engineering performed had a linear range of 0.1 to 10 μM and a LOD of 0.1 μM. Using macrocycle molecules such as cyclodextrins, cucurbit[n]urils, calix[n]arenes, and pillar[n]arenes, functional supramolecular systems can be built that take advantage of the host–guest recognition. Due to their symmetrical pillar topologies, electron-donating cavities and ease of functionalization, pillar[n]arenes, primarily pillar[5]arenes and pillar[6]arenes have been extensively researched. Shi and colleagues [141] reported extremely selective strategy for detection of l -Asp using fluorescent NPs produced via a water-soluble pillar[5]arene (WP5)-based host–guest interaction. The extremely specific detection of Asp resulted in the transformation of fluorescent NPs into nanosheets with weak fluorescence emission after the addition of l-Asp to a solution containing fluorescent NPs which LOD for l -Asp were determined to be 0.209 μM. The special presence of NPs has left its impact on the obtained data. Karami and colleagues [142] applied a one-step hydrothermal treatment of 3-nitroaniline and glucose in sulfuric acid solution and prepared an unique inherent dual-emitting carbon dots (CDs) which used for ratiometric detection of Asp. At pH 4.0, Asp showed a better performance than other AAs in restoring the CDs-Cu2+ system's quenched fluorescence. This ON-OFF fluorescence based optical sensing method allowed for the quantification of Asp in the linear concentration range of 0.2 to 15 μM and a LOD of 0.1 μM using a selective ratiometric fluorescence technique. Due to notable characteristics such as high chemo/photo-stability, fast and inexpensive synthesis, minimal cytotoxicity, simplicity and great water solubility of surface modification, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn significant consideration as a novel class of metal-free fluorescent nanomaterials. In an optical study developed by Guan and coworkers [143] the superquenching exclusivity of AuNPs to polythiophene derivatives were used in order to detect the target molecule (Asp). The architected sensor was tested for the detection of Asp in a linear range of 0.075 to 6 μM, and LOD of 0.032 μM. Polythiophenes in their oxidized states exhibit a special conductivity. Combination of polythiophenes with nanoparticles that have excellent electron transfer properties, it leads to the creation of this sensitive approach (excellent analytical data). As a result, the sensor developed by Guan and coworkers had shown an excellent analytical result. Finally, for the further development of these sensors, it is suggested to use silicate nanomaterials, metallic nanomaterials and carbon-based nanomaterials, especially QDs with excellent optical properties.

Combining other materials with NPs helps to create selective and sensitive sensors for the determination of molecules in a wide dynamic range. Different types of sensors can be modified with most NPs and the output signal can be increased. Among the investigated methods, MMN, MWCNTs, AuNPs and CDs nanomaterials have been used frequently. However, other such as silicate nanomaterials, metallic NPs, and carbon-based nanomaterials can be used in the development of these sensors. Among the above reports, the EC method has brought better analytical data. Entirely, the reported studies for EC and fluorescence sensors had detection ranges of 0.37 nM to 1.77 μM and 0.032 to 0.209 μM, respectively. For Asp monitoring, EC and fluorescence-based measurement were found to be efficient. However, there is a deep lack of ECL, PEC, SPR, FRET and piezoelectric methods, which is a clear research gap that needs to be addressed. Also, there is no report for the biosensing of Asp using bioreceptors such as enzyme, aptamer, DNA, and monoclonal antibodies. Thus, biotechnologist suggested that work in this area of research towards preparing new aptamer for specific surveillance in real samples. Finally, we propose the design of new portable and cheap sensors.

In cellular metabolism, glutamic acid (GA) is a key compound that affects cognitive function, such as memory, learning, and brain development, regulates growth cones, and synaptogenesis [144,145]. According to previous reports, L-GA stimulates dopamine release spontaneously by glutamatergic neurons controlling nerve terminals directly. GA is found as a flavor enhancer and nutrient in all dairy products, poultry, meat, fish, and eggs, as well as foods such as wheat and rice (as protein-rich plant foods) [146,147]. Consequently, specific recognition of GA is essential to ensure the quality of life. So, in this section various types of sensors/biosensors for the monitoring of GA were surveyed.

One of the stimulants in the nervous system, which is also a transmitter, is called glutamate. Genetically engineered periplasmic glutamate-binding protein (GluBP)-based sensors are used for target specificity. Zeynaloo and colleagues [148] developed an EC biosensor to quantify GA by covalently immobilizing a periplasmic glutamate-binding protein engineered on an Au nanoparticle- SPCE (GluBP/AuNP/SPCE). The LOD was 0.15 μM with linear range of 0.1–0.8 μM.

Due to their remarkable biocompatibility and simplicity of conjugation with biomolecules, Au NPs have been extensively employed for the construction of EC sensors. Wei and colleagues [149] suggested a novel interface based on l-histidine-regulated zeolitic imidazolate framework (l -His-ZIF) which used for the effective, easy, and enantioselective measurement of L-GA using EIS technique. The l-His-ZIF interface may be employed for chiral identification without the usage of high-conductive chemicals, unlike MOF-involved voltammetry, which requires numerous immobilizations. The enantioselective detection of L-GA was effectively performed using the l-His-ZIF interface, in the linear range of 0.1 to 50 nM and a LOD of 0.06 nM.

In a study conducted by Alam et al., [150] RuO2-doped ZnO NPs modified GCE was utilized for the sensitive detection of GA. By evaluating the analytical parameters obtained by this sensor for the detection of L-GA, (the LOD of 96 ± 5 pM, the response time of 20 s, good stability, and repeatability in phosphate buffer solution) we can say that this strategy is an ideal choice for reliable recognition of this analyte.

In another similar example, Alam et al., [151] synthesized ZnO/NiO/Al2O3 NPs simply and applied a thin layer of these NPs on GCE using 5 % Nafion glue. ZnO/NiO/Al2O3 NPs/GCE was able to set a LOD of 95.35 pM toward detection of L-GA. Scientifically and theoretically, semiconductor nano-metal oxides have recently attracted much attention, when their EC properties are enhanced by doping with other metal oxides. For example, ZnO can be widely used in the structure of sensors. p-type semiconductors (ZnO) with excellent stability, high electronic/photonic properties have been considered. In addition, employing another p-type metal oxide semiconductor (NiO) can be effective on the performance of sensors. Finally, Al2O3 is preferred in design of sensors with its attractive photoelectronic features. In 2019, Zeng and colleagues [152] used a sensor to differentiate GA enantiomers. They achieved the desired sensor by electropolymerization of the 2,5-dimethoxyaniline (PDMA) on the GCE surface. Using this sensor, the LOD was 0.26 mM for D-GA and 0.11 mM for L-GA. The above development has its value because it was able to recognize both enantiomers of the target molecule. The system proposed by Wei Group is preferred due to its better structural, chemical, and analytical properties. The absence of biosensors is visible, so the use of aptamers, enzymes, DNA and peptides are recommended for improvement of GA’s sensors performance. In addition, the use of hybrid-nanomaterials, silicate NPs, polymeric nanocomposites and carbon-based nanomaterials is suggested.

A member of the catecholamine family that acts as a neurotransmitter is dopamine. Dopamine can be oxidized to form polydopamine. Polydopamine is used in optical systems as a quencher in biological studies. For example, Ci and coworkers [153] designed a fluorescent nano-switch system to determine GA in living cells and human serum. This approach was based on fluorescent polydopamine dots (PDADs), that precursor is called dopamine. This sensor has a LOD of 0.12 µM for GA in the concentration range of 0 to 100 µM. Recently, Yuan et al., [154] engineered a sensor using dual-emission carbon dots (CD) that had orange fluorescence. The above approach was based on the change of fluorescence intensity. This architecture showed a LOD of 0.085 μM in the linear range 0 to 400 μM. In another example, Zhang et al [155], developed a fluorescence probe based on Fe-MIL-88NH2 nanohybrid with a regular octahedral structure toward detection of GA. This nano hybrid fluorescence probe applied for the determination of GA in the range of 0 to 100 μM with LOD of 2.04 μM. Also, due to its good fluorescence property, it recorded a suitable response for measuring GA in heLa cells. Due to the unique chemical and physical properties of MOFs, hybrid materials with the properties like optical, easy modification and tunable pore structure are recognized as an important material in the probe’s construction toward detection of various analytes. Among the three reviewed studies, the second example had better performance. In this section, according to the fluorescence sensors for the determination of GA, it is clear that few nanomaterials have been used and no biological agents have been used.

Among all the reports summarized in Table 4 , the analytical results related to the EC sensor have more efficient and better response. The suitable analytical results can be confidently attributed to the very effective presence of NPs. In general, the use of AuNP, RuO2-ZnO, ZnO/NiO/Al2O3, CD NPs, and Fe-MIL-88NH2 nanohybrid are reported. However, carbon nanomaterials, new silicate NPs (such as DFNS) and metal NPs can be further explored in this field. Also, the presence of biological elements in EC and fluorescence sensor shows the relatively high desire of researchers to use them. Therefore, it is expected that researchers will pay more attention to the use of aptamers, peptides, genes and enzymes in the design of new platforms. According to the results obtained by fluorescence sensors, it can be understood that compared to EC sensors, they do not have very good analytical results, and the necessity of more precision is felt in this field. By examining the reported studies, it is known that for EC and fluorescence sensors, the detection range of 0.00009535 to 260 μM and 0.085to 2.04 μM was obtained, respectively. On the other hand, unfortunately, no specific research was found for piezoelectric, ECL, PEC, SPR and FRET sensors, which we hope will solve this research gap. Also, there is no report on the biosensing of this analyte which is important research gap for the specific analysis of GA in real sample. Finally, we expect that in addition to meeting the mentioned needs, simple, affordable and portable sensors will also be developed.

Arginine (Arg) is the only AA that contains the guanidino group in its structure [156]. This AA plays a critical role in the synthesis of polyamines, glutamate, creatine, NO, and proline [157]. Arg great effect on human brain chemistry, including neurotransmission, vasodilation, and immune responses, attracted the attention of researchers [158]. Same as other AAs, except glycine, Arg is a chiral molecule [159]. l-Arg acts as an energy transfer biological probe and is known as a semi-essential AA. It helps the body cope with physiological stress, lowers ammonia toxicity in the kidneys, and regulates pathological activities. ]n the body, l-Arg helps to wound healing, nitrogen balance, gene expression control, autoimmunity (immune system), and hormone function. As a result, a precise and sensitive diagnosis of Arg is critical in medicine [160]. Therefore, in this section various types of sensors and biosensors on the efficient identification of Arg are surveyed. Also, advantages and limitations of these methods (EC, optical, PEC, ECLو and etc) are discussed carefully.

Compared to enzyme-based sensors, aptamer-based biosensors are more stable. Aptamers have the characteristic to identify the target molecule in a specific way. Therefore, the use of aptamers in the development of sensors can create high sensitivity and specificity. In a study by He and his colleagues [161], molecular docking was used to screen for the binding ability of peptide aptamer AA. They confirmed the accuracy of this by isothermal titration calorimetry. Once modified, the peptide was used as a diagnostic element for sensor development. The change in peptide structure by l-Arg was the reason for its modification. The prepared sensor showed a linear range of 0.1 pM to 0.1 mM and a LOD of 0.01 pM. Experiments were also carried out on actual specimens (porcine serum). However, it turned out that more effort was needed to improve the sensitivity of these samples. Zhu and colleagues [162] proposed an enzyme-free amperometric sensor based on a heptadecapeptide with an electroactive ferrocene (Fc) linker as ferrocene-Gly-Gly-Gly-Phe-Gly-His-Ile-His-Glu-Gly-Tyr-Gly-Gly-Gly-Lys-(CH2)4-dithiocyclopentane toward recognition of l -Arg. The suggested approach can be used as a “Signal-ON” sensor for detecting l -Arg at concentrations as low as 31 pM, with a linear range of 0.0001 to 10 μM. With a recovery rate of 97.5–106.9 %, the Fc-conjugated specific peptide sensor was successfully used to determine l -Arg in pig serums, and the test findings were in good agreement with those of the chromatographic equipment. The Fc-conjugated specific peptide with unusual shape and middle-recessed spherical particles facilitates its binding to the target (l-Arg), resulting in highly selective detection of l -Arg using this peptide-based biosensors. Another recent work has been done by Shantharaja and coworkers [163]. First, the precursor protocol for the synthesis of cobalt (II) phthalocyanine analog pyrazolopyrimidine polymer (poly-CoTPzPyPc) was used. They proved the successful synthesis of the polymer with several analytical techniques. The proposed system showed a linear range of 10 to 100 μM and a LOD of 2.5 μM. Also in ChA, it had a linear range of 2–60 μM with LOD of 0.6 μM. The platform was also showed significant recovery values for urine sample evaluation.

In 2020, an EC biosensor was designed for rapid detection of l-Arg in leukemic blood samples [164]. In this work, ADI (enzyme Arg deiminase)/Fe3O4/MWCNT/PANI (polyaniline) were placed on the GCE surface and the biosensor was prepared. According to this report, LOD was 20 μM. The selectivity of this system was also successful in determining the target in the real sample. The presence of a thin layer of ADI/Fe3O4/MWCNT/PANI nanocomposite improve the performance significantly. However, this method did not show high accuracy. The system presented by reserch group of Prof. He showed a more sensitive reaction than other methods, which could be due to the use of aptamer in their system. Aptamers can specifically identify the target, and this is a good property that has shown its effects well in this research [146]. Exploring the literature shows that nanomaterials have not been extensively used in electrochemical studies, therefore, it is anticipated that presence of nanomaterials can EC provide sensors with greater sensitivity and specificity. Silicate nanomaterials, metallic nanomaterials (like CdSe, ZnS, AuNPs, Ni and CuNPs) and carbon-based nanomaterials (SWCNTs, MWCNTs, rGO, GO and GQDs) can be used to develop EC sensors of Arg in the future. It is also recommended to use the excellent properties of biological agents and design immune-, geno- and aptasensor for the EC determination of Arg, efficiently.

Glutathione is a tripeptide composed of glycine, Cys and GA. Glutathione can create specificity in analyte detection and therefore has been taken into consideration for fabrication of sensors. In 2018, Liu et al [165] synthesized glutathione-coated Au and silver nanoclusters (GSH-Au/AgNCs) to develop an Arg-sensitive fluorescence sensor. GSH-Au/AgNCs as a sensitive optical probe showed a LOD of 0.25 µM in the concentration range of 0 to 100 µM. The developed probe was able to record this function due to the presence of NPs and glutathione in its structure. He and colleagues [166] successfully developed an optical sensor consisted of a bis-rhodamine-thiourea/Al3+ complex for sensitive detection of Arg. This sensor in aquatic environments showed relatively good selectivity among other interfering factors to Arg. The above sensor can change the fluorescence intensity and color change visible to the naked eye within 1 min due to the opening and closing of the spiro ring in rhodamine. The linear detection range was 0 to 0.12 mM with LOD of 2.3 μM. One of the ideal species used to detect analytes in aqueous media is metal ion complexes.

In an optical study, Pu et al., [169] developed a probe based on the aggregation of citrate-coated gold nanoparticles for fast and simple visual detection of Arg in real samples. By examining this probe using UV/Vis technique, it was found that it has a LOD of 16 nM in the concentration range of 0.08 to 13.2 μM. This method was able to identify Arg easily and without the need for complex tools, which, of course, is mainly due to the extraordinary role of Au NPs. Pu group system showed better sensitivity to target analysis than the previous method. Undoubtedly, the role of colored NPs is important in obtaining a better analytical answer, so we suggest the use of nanoparticles with optical properties. On the other hand, the use of aptamers, peptides, antibodies, and enzymes along with nanomaterials can lead to the creation of new and efficient sensors for the sensitive determination of analyte.

Recently, Fan and Wang [170] engineered an SPR sensor for the sensitive determination of Arg. In this work, they used molecular self-assembly technology to modify the 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA) molecule on the sensor surface. The system is based on the hydrogen bond between Arg and MUA. The prepared probe in the concentration range of 5 to 500 nM succeeded in identifying Arg with a LOD of 0.005 µM. The above approach showed a suitable analytical result with suitable sensitivity and simplicity.

One of the main challenges in the field of analyte detection is the design of sensitive, fast and specific sensors. FERT-based sensors are one of the good and promising sensing tools that have become popular in the determination of different analytes. FRET is a non-radiative procedure into which the electronic incitement zing of a donor (usually a fluorophore) is transferred to a basis case receiver through dipole–dipole interactions. In 2019, Wu et al., [171] proposed a FRET type bio-probe for detection of Arg. In this architecture, negatively charged AuNP acts as energy acceptor, and positively charged up conversion phosphor NaYF4 (NYF) acts as energy donor. The acceptor and oppositely charged donor come into close vicinity via electrostatic attraction efficacy, which outcome in the occurrence of FRET amongst AuNP and NYF. The FRET procedure is thus in the “Signal-ON” mood, meantime the system is into the “OFF” mood, and the emitting light of NYF quenched. After the addition of the target molecule (Arg) which has a positive charge, guanidyl of Arg binds to AuNP and changes the charge and separates AuNP from NYF due to electrostatic repulsion. The FRET procedure is blocked and the approach switches to the “signal-ON” expression since the interval between AuNP and NYF becomes longer. This system brought a LOD of 2.9 μM in a linear range of 14.42 to 115.04 μM. The effect of AuNP in this optical approach is clear as we have already discussed about the excellent properties of these particles. In a research of FRET type, Ramírez-Herrera and coworkers [172] determined Arg, which is based on inhibition of FRET efficiency in complexes between cresyl violet dye (as an energy acceptor) and CdTe quantum dots coated with mercaptopropionic acid (QD/MPA) (as a source of energy). After the addition of Arg to the medium, a displacement of cresyl violet occurred on the QD/MPA surface, which caused the FRET between cresyl violet and QD/MPA to be interrupted, and the fluorescence emission of the donor was restored. This approach had a linear range of 1 to 30 μM with LOD of 0.51 μM. This fluorescence nano-probe has low cost, significant speed and good selectivity towards Arg. In another example, Li et al., [173] developed a carbon nanodots (CDs-1) optical hybrid nano-approach for the sensitive measurement of Arg, which had a LOD of 65 nM in the concentration range of 60 μM to 0.27 mM. Also, it could selectively detect intracellular Arg and locate mitochondrion. Considering that CDs-1 has limitations such as low thermal stability, hybrid material integrated with rapid organic synthesis methods can be utilized.

In 2018, in order to detect chiral enantiomers of Arg, Yuan and colleagues [174] designed an optical biosensor that had aptamer and Au NPs in its structure. In this approach, carboxyfluorescein (FAM)-labeled aptamer were absorbed on Au NPs, where the fluorescence intensity was quenched by Au NPs based on FRET method. This optical probe had a linear range of 0 to 300 nM and 0 to 400 nM with respect to D- and L- Arg and also had a LOD of 1.8 nM.

Among the four existing methods, the last method was more efficient than the other three methods. Here, the achievement of a better response can be related to the presence of the aptamer because the aptamers create a completely specific function in the sensor and are generally effective in achieving a better signal. As a result, we suggest witnessing the creation of new platforms using NPs such as QDs, Ag, Au and Pt, along with biological agents such as aptamer. Table 5 summarized different types of sensors and biosensor of Arg. Among the studied biosensors, it is obvious that the peptide-based sensor has the lowest LOD among all samples. This is a good indication that biological agents such as aptamers are extremely effective in creating a highly sensitive sensor. So, the development of new biosensors using aptamers, peptides, and enzymes is recommended. Meanwhile, in most of the studies, nanomaterials (MWCNT, Au/AgNCs, CDs and AuNPs) are intelligently present. Additionally, other nanomaterials such as Si, Cu, TiO2, Ag, Pd NPs and GQDs, CNTs, rGO, carbon black and graphene nanomaterials have the potential to be used in sensors. Among the available data, as mentioned, the EC based aptasensors had the best response (0.00001 to 20000 nM). Also, fluorescence-based methods showed the detection range of 16 to 2300 nM, SPR with LOD of 5 nM and FRET based methods had a linear range of 1.8 to 2900 nM for this analyte. Considering the importance of identifying Arg and further development of analytical tools, it is expected that ECL, PEC and piezoelectric sensors will also be developed. Finally, we enthusiastically suggest that researchers develop portable and stable sensors using various nanomaterials and biological agents to determine Arg.

l-Lysine is an essential AA that cannot be produced in the body and must be obtained through a balanced diet [175]. Lysine (Lys) assists the body in absorbing calcium and is also necessary for normal cell growth [25]. Lys plays an effective role in making collagen to build teeth, bones, cartilage, ligaments, and skin. Damaged skin (bruises), broken bones, blood vessels, as well as teeth sticking to the gums require collagen. In addition, Lys has antiviral effects and slows the progression of the dangerous HIV in the body. People with anemia, nausea, loss of appetite, growth retardation, and reproductive disorders may not have enough Lys in their diet [176]. Due to the important role of Lys in the human health, its accurate diagnosis is crucial. In this section, the role of detection method and type of nanomaterials on the efficiency of sensors performance for Lys monitoring are surveyed. Also, advantages and limitation of these methods are discussed.

Lysine oxidase (LyOx) is a protein belonging to the family of AA oxidases which specifically catalyzes l-Lys. Chauhan et al., [177] used a commercial LyOx that was stabilized by covalent bonding on PtNPs and AuNPs and casted on the Au electrode using 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (3-APTES) and glutaraldehyde cross-linking chemistry. The biosensor designed to detect l-Lys exhibited a linear range of 1 to 600 μM and a LOD of 1 μM. A suitable surface for biomolecules is provided by AuNPs. They also provide good conductivity between biomolecules and electrodes and a high surface area. On the other hand, PtNPs are superior to noble metal NPs and have good electrocatalytic properties against oxidation/reduction of H2O2. In an EC design, Huang and coworkers [178] developed a sensitive sensor based on a nanostructured composite (NC) to measure Lys enantiomers. The NC has been successfully synthesized via covalent coupling between thionine (Thi), chiral chooser L-N-tert-butoxycarbonyl-O-benzylserine (l-BBSer) and the semiconductor 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA). To perform this measurement, they studied the GCE modified with NC using the EIS method. This system provided a good linear response in the range of 100 nM to 10 mM and a LOD of 33 nM. In this approach, the NC is a clever development due to the presence of PTCA as an electronic agent, Thi as a redox probe, and l -BBSer as a chiral selector. In another example, Gholivand and coworkers [179] developed an enzyme-free system based on titanium oxide NPs and MWCNTs to detect Lys on a GCE substrate. Using MWCNT- TiO2NPs and EC techniques the detection linear range was 5 to 110 μM and the LOD was 4 μM through CV technique and the dynamic range was 500 to 5500 nM and the LOD was 390 nM via DPV technique. While MWCNTs have interesting mechanical, electrical, and geometric properties, the presence of TiO2NPs can have a modifying function that further improves biocompatibility, optical transparency, and conductivity of MWCNTs.

Recently, Nohwal et al., [180] successfully developed an amperometric bio-sensor to detect l-Lys. They fixed graphene oxide NPs (GrONPs) and L- Lys oxidase NPs (LOxNPs) on a PGE to design a sensor. Different decomposition stages (LOxNPs/GrONPs/PGE) were examined by CV. The developed biosensor had a linear range of 0.01 to 1000 μM and a LOD of 0.01 μM. The sensor showed a quick response time (3.95 s) and the stability of 180 days. Due to the thermal, optical, mechanical, and electrical characteristics of graphene based nanomaterials, they are used in various systems. Among the examined examples, the analytical results of Nehwal group were more efficient than other researches. Due to the lack of biological factors such as aptamer, peptide, enzyme and gene in this section, it is expected that action will be taken to cover this gap.

Ovalbumin (OVA) is a rich, inexpensive, and comparatively well-studied source of glycoprotein. In 2018, Zhang et al., [181] developed a new sensor for the sensitive measurement of l-Lys using OVA. For the architecture of this system, they used copper nanocluster (CuNCs) probe with OVA, which had the role of stabilizer. According to this report, l-Lys increased the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe, which indicates the sensitivity of the sensor to the target molecule. This probe and sensing system showed a linear range of 10 μM to 1 mM with LOD of 5.5 μM. Also, it was successful in measuring l-Lys in urine samples. Cu is abundant, cheap, and readily available compared to Ag, Au and other noble metals. Therefore, CuNCs are being followed with interest in recent analytical studies. For example, Bhosale and coworkers [182] designed a fluorescence and colorimetric sensor for rapid detection of Lys in aqueous conditions. As Lys was gradually added, the peak absorption of the UV–vis spectrum 8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonic acid trisodium salt (HPTS) probe increased. The LOD of this system for determination of Lys was 3.106 μM. HPTS has been shown to identify Lys in the presence of different AAs in interferometric investigations. The existence of the acidic OH group in the HPTS probe was essential for the detection of Lys, which was confirmed by adsorption studies..

In another work developed by Zhang and colleagues [183], a fluorescence-based optical nanosensor capable of switching cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs) was developed for detection of Lys. The linear range of this off–on nanosensor was 2 to 200 μM, with a LOD of 0.146 μM. QDs are preferred in most optical sensors due to properties such as long fluorescence lifetime, adjustable diffusion, wide absorption, and high quantum efficiency.

Zhu and coworkers [184] developed a fluorescent diarylethene-based sensor by binding triazole to a methyl quinoline unit with a photochromic diarylethene. The designed system demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity to Lys. This optical sensor had a LOD of 0.019 μM. Fast response, thermal stability, and good fatigue resistance are some of the interesting properties of diarylethene, which is known as a photochromic compound and isone of themaximumadvantages among photochromic materials.

Interestingly, Yu et al., [185] synthesized and used AuNCs using papaya juice (P- AuNCs) as a reducing agent and cap to develop a fluorescence sensor for the detection of l-Lys in real samples. P-AuNCs has good biocompatibility and high stability. In addition, linear range of 10 to 1000 μM and LOD of 6 μM were obtained by this sensor. In addition, the system based on P-AuNCs was used to monitor the contents of l-Lys in urine samples. According to the explanations given and having better analytical results than other methods, the work of Zhu's group is preferable. Owing to the lack of biological factors like aptamer, peptide, enzyme, and gene in this section, it is expected that action will be taken to cover this gap. Finally, the development of simple and affordable sensors that can detect Lys with a smartphone without the need for sophisticated equipment is expected in near future.

Oxidases are enzymes that catalyze oxidation reactions and are the specific agents of the sensor in identifying a specific analyte. In 2003, Marquette and colleagues [186] developed a biological assay (chip) for detecting target molecules according to the ECL detection of enzymatically synthesized hydrogen peroxide. They fixed Lys non-covalently on diethyl aminoethyl (DEAE) anion converter beads or iminodiacetic acid (GOD only) chelating beads. A glassy carbon foil that trapped in PVA-SbQ photopolymer was discolored even further. Chip measurements were performed when a potential of 850 mV was applied between the quasi-platinum reference and glass carbon foil for 3 min. Whereas a numeric picture of the bio-sensor chip was taken with a camera (CCD). To achieve spatial signals, the utilization of luminol grains (DEAE/Sepharose) in the sensor layer is clearly defined. The LOD of this ECL-based sensor for determination of Lys was about 1 μM and the linear detection range was equal to 1 μM to 0.5 mM. In another example, Wang et al [187] used an ECL sensor for the quantitative determination of Lys in the presence of tris (2,2ʹ-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) in an alkaline buffer solution (Na2CO3-NaHCO3) and on an indium tin oxide (ITO) glass as substrate. The applied technique was flow injection. In the meantime, to dominate the disadvantages of traditional cells, a novel ECL-based cytosensor has been conceived. A quantitative analysis of l-Lys by this biosensor were 1.43 ng.mL −1 as LOD and 10 ng.ml −1 to 0.1 mg.ml −1 as linear range. It is obvious that, the Wang Group system is better than the first study due to its outstanding inherent properties and ability to create ECL with an extensive range of analytes (such as AA, amines, oxalate, and NADH) by tris (2,2ʹ-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+). In addition, good electrical conductivity is due to the presence of ITO, which has caused a relatively high sensitivity.

In this subsection, all the sensors and biosensors that developed to detection of Lys were summarized in Table 6 and hire analytical performance were compared. As seen in most researches, the presence of different nanomaterials/NPs (AuNPs, PtNPs, MWCNTs, TiO2, GrONPs, LOxNPs, CuNCs, CdTe QDs and AuNCs) is quite clear. In addition to the used nanomaterials, we suggest the use of silicate nanomaterials such as DFNS, MCM-41, and hybrid NPs. Nano materials both with their optical and electrical properties lead to better results.
