Also, the presence of biological compounds in the research causes the special performance of that sensor in relation to the analyte, so it is hoped that the unique properties of biological compounds (such as aptamer, peptide, gene and enzyme) will be used more in future systems. Almost equal number of researches has been done between EC and fluorescence methods, but few ECL sensors were found. However, this deficiency is expected to be overcome as the ECL sensor has a better response. In total, the reported studies for EC, fluorescence and ECL sensors had detection ranges of 10 nM to 4000 nM, 0.019 to 6 μM and 1.43 to 1 μM, respectively. However, there are a few research on SPR, FRET, PEC and piezoelectric sensorsand there are high demand in strengthening these areas. Finally, simple sensors with more sensitivity and stability can be developed for the specific detection of Lys so that health status can be obtained without going to a medical center.

Histidine (His) is one of the most important AAs in body, which serves as construction blocks in protein. His is known as a neurotransmitter and plays a critical function in the body's biological systems. To highlight some of its roles, it can be said that it minimizes the internal bleeding caused by micro trauma and reduces the risk. His also acts as a metal transfer controller in biological fluids [188,189]. Lack of His in human body can lead to weight loss and also if the diet is not eaten enough, it can disrupt the balance of nitrogen in the body. His deficient can cause liver cirrhosis, rheumatoid arthritis, and nerve deafness [190]. On the other hand, excess His may cause stress and anxiety, the intoxication of thrombotic disorders, and schizophrenia [191]. For these reasons, its precise and rapid determination is important for health, clinical treatment, and research. In this section different types of sensing methods for the identification of His are investigated. In addition, role of nanomaterials on the increment of analytical performance of biosensors of His are surveyed. Finally, a critical comparative of methods is done.
Aptamers have attracted a lot of attention recently; more details about this biological element were discussed earlier in sub-section 2.5.1.

Liang et al., [192] created an aptasensor based on the switching structure of aptamer and Au NPs–graphene nanosheets (GNPs–GNSs) nanocomposites for the primary time. Initially, GNPs immobilized on the GNSs surface by sodium citrate through chemical reduction of chloroauric acid. In GCE modified by GNPs–GNSs composite, the ferrocene (Fc) redox signaling element was brought closer to the electrode surface to produce measurable signals. The designed sensor showed a wide linear range from 10 pM to 10 μM. In addition, a low LOD of 0.1 pM for His was obtained by this sensor.

In 2018, Xu and coworkers [193] designed a new sensitive platform for the determination of His. For this purpose, 3D porous Ni foam with supreme electro-catalytic activity was used. As a result, a sensitive and simple sensor with a linear range of 0.15 to 20.3 μM and a LOD of 50 nM was engineered. Also, identification was performed in 0.1 M NaOH solution. This porous Ni foam is cost-effective and commercially available.

In another research work, Zhang and colleagues [194] designed a sensitive EC sensor for detection of l -His by combining MWCNTs and a molecular printing layer. In this system, the ITO electrode was used as a substrate. The substrate was modified with MWNTs and then with a thin layer of molecularly imprinted polymers. According to this report, the linear range of 2 μM to 1 mM and LOD of 5.8 nM were obtained. MWCNTs in the above study have also attracted the attention of researchers to increase sensitivity.

In another study, Pei et al., [195] modified GCE with copper germanate nanowires (firmly attached to the electrode surface) and applied for His determination. The developed sensor displayed a linear relationship between 0.005 and 2 mM and a LOD of 1.3 μM. Copper nanowires with large surface area, good electrical properties, and small size appear promising in electrode modification.

In 2013, Chen and others [196] synthesized nickel hydroxide hourglass-shaped nanostructure to make an EC sensor. The synthesis method of this nanostructure was simple and easy in solution. They fixed the nickel hydroxide nanostructure on the surface of electrode. This system showed a linear range of 0.1 μM to 0.5 mM and a LOD of 80 nM in NaOH (0.1 M) solution. The designed system could determine the His in-human blood serum. It also showed good selectivity in the presence of interfering agents. The simplicity of the nanostructures synthesis and their properties are interesting.

In conclusion, as can be seen, research group of Liang's has a lower LOD than other works. Finally, we recommend the use of hybrid and silicate nanomaterials, as well as the use of metal NPs such as CuNPs, TiO2, AuNPs, AgNPs, and PdNPs), along with biological agents such as DNA, aptamer, enzyme, and gene. It is also necessary to develop sensors that can diagnose His without going to clinics or diagnostic laboratories.

In 2015, Wang et al., [197] designed a sensitive fluorescent sensor to detect l
-His in real samples. This sensor was prepared based on the restriction of double-stranded DNA (ds-DNA) templated copper NPs (Cu NPs) by l-His. The strong interaction between the imidazole in His with Cu2+ groups enabled the unlabeled sensor to pinpoint the target molecule. In addition, this interaction reduces the fluorescence intensity in the existence of other AAs. This interaction also prevents fluorescent copper from forming effectively. The system had a concentration range of 0.2 to 100 μM and LOD of 20 nM. The system did not require labeling and other complex operations and was synthesized within 5 min. The good biocompatibility, excellent fluorescence properties, as well as lower toxicity are advantages of this approach.

In 2017, Wang and Fan [198] developed a fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs)-Cu2+. In this sensor, binding between Cu2+ and N-GQDs quench the fluorescence of the N-GQDs. However, due to the high affinity of His for Cu2+, it separates Cu2+ from N-GQDs and recover the fluorescence again. This optical probe demonstrated a linear range of 0 to 35 μM and LOD of 72.2 nM compared to the target. GQDshave led to the creation of efficient optical sensors due to their appealing electrical and optical features. Also, in this system, due to the N doping with GQDs, the optical properties were significantly increased. In addition, this environmentally friendly design is cost-effective and simple.

In another example, He and coworkers [199] developed a DNAzyme fluorescent sensor based on a hybridization chain reaction (HCR) for sensitive detection of l -His. In this research work, l-His allowed the efficient alteration of DNAzyme to produce gaped Sub in minutes. It was activated by hybridization chain reaction (HCR) as a functional activator to release G-quadruplex and long binary strands. They played a role in improving the accuracy of G-quadruplex chains for the performance of zinc porphyrin (ZnPPIX) as a scaffold. The above sensor demonstrated a linear range of 5 nM to 5 μM and a LOD of 2.7 nM. It is a sensitive and flexible platform that can also detect the target (His) in urine samples. This sensor can selectively detect His in the presence of other biological targets.

In another work, He et al., [200] used Ni2+-modified Au nanoclusters (AuNCs) as a His sensing sensor. They first quenched the Au NCs fluorescence with Ni2+ and then recovered the fluorescence by adding His. From this system, a concentration range of 0.1 to 26 μM and a LOD of 30 nM were obtained. Unlike metal-based QDs, which are relatively toxic heavy metals that are relatively large (3 to 100 nm) in size and difficult to synthesize, metal nanoclusters are viable alternatives. Advantages such as small size, good light stability, easy synthesis, and most importantly low toxicity make them a very fine candidate for fluorescence sensors. This system showed good biocompatibility alone with good selectivity for the target (His) in the existence of interrupting agents (e.g., other AAs). Recently, Chu and colleagues [201] designed a fluorescence sensor using copper-based metal–organic framework NPs (Cu-MOFs)/Fe3+ to measure His in real samples. Fe3+ acts as a fluorescence quencher and Cu-MOFs act as a fluorescence source (with blue diffusion). It should be noted that Fe3+ quenched the blue emission of Cu-MOFs by creating photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between Fe3+ and Cu-MOFs. In the presence of His in the sensing system, the chelation between Fe3+ and l-His overcome the interaction between Fe3+ with Cu-MOFs. As a result, it restrained PET of Cu-MOFs to Fe3+ to recover fluorescence. This sensor has a concentration range of 1 to 190 μM and a LOD of 156 nM. In this method, the increase of sensing capacity and accuracy occurred due to the use of Cu-MOFs, which is due to its good interaction with guest molecules. This method was also used as a target measuring probe in serum samples. The designed sensor by He group [200] showed a better LOD than other techniques. Finally, we recommend the use of silicate nanomaterials and hybrid, as well as the use of metal NPs such as TiO2, CuNPs, AuNPs,Pd NPs and AgNPs), along with biological agents such as gene, DNA, aptamer and enzyme for developing sensors with higher selectively and sensitivity.

Recently, Dong and coworkers [202] developed a FRET-type optical sensor based on the off–on optical technique for the determination of l-His. N-acetyl-L Cys functionalized Au nanoclusters (NAC-AuNCs) and S, P, N co-doped carbon dots (S, P, N-CDs) are used in the FRET approach, which serve as acceptor and energy donor, respectively. Adding NAC-AuNCs in the dilution of S, P, N-CDs quenched the fluorescence of S, P, N -CDs efficiently but the far-red fluorescence of NAC-AuNCs appeared. However, the fluorescence was restored upon l-His addition. This system showed a LOD of 0.374 µM in the concentration range of 3.13 to 56.25 µM. Also, this approach has been successfully used in real examples. Unfortunately, not only FRET-based optical sensors have not investigated extensively but also none of the biological factors such as aptamers, enzymes, genes, peptides, and cell base have been used in FRET-based sensors of l-His. Carbon-based nanomaterials such as MWCNTs, SWCNTs, GQDs, rGO and carbon black and metal NPs such as CuNPs, TiO2, AuNPs, AgNPs, and Pd NPs can be used in future researches for fabrication of more advanced FRET-based sensors.

In Table 7 , all of EC and optical sensors/biosensors for the detection of l-His are collected. Although several studies have been found on biosensors, there is still a lack of immunosensors and genes for His detection. Researchers intelligently have used NPs (GNPs–GNSs, MWNTs, copper germanate nanowires, Cu NPs, GQDs and Au NCs) to achieve more sensitive responses. In addition to the above nanomaterials, metal nanoparticles such as TiO2, Pd NPs, ZnONPs, Fe3O4, and silicate NPs can be used for further progress. For His, EC and fluorescence methods are well developed, but only one study was found for the FRET technique. In total, the reported studies for EC, fluorescence, and FRET sensors had detection ranges of 0.0001 to 1300 nM, 2.7 to 156 nM and 374 nM, respectively. On the other hand, a platform for SPR, piezoelectric, PEC, and ECL methods has not been developed till now. Finally, we predict that simple, small, stable and portable chips are developed using nanotechnology and biotechnology to measure His.

One of the essential non-polar aromatic and hydrophobic AAs is phenylalanine (Phe). It acts as a precursor for a vital AA (i.e. tyrosine and its ramification). It is utilized in food manufacturing as an additive (aspartame and sweetener) and in pharmaceutic manufacturing to synthesize some antibiotics such as gramicidin, bacitracin, and tyrocidine [203,204]. Phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) in the human body converts this aromatic AA into tyrosine. It should be noted that the precursor of monoamine neurotransmitters associated with apathy and depression is tyrosine. Noradrenaline and adrenaline are also synthesized from dopamine, in which Phe plays an important role. The important role of Phe in anxiety, mood, and depression has been confirmed [203–205]. Therefore, accurate measurement of Phe is one of the most important works for mental and physical health.In this part of review, various types of sensors and biosensors for the Phe analysis are discussed. Also, role of advanced nanomaterials for the recognition of this AA are investigated. Finally, advantages and limitation of methods and materials are surveyed.

The Phe dehydrogenase enzyme, which belongs to the oxidoreductase enzyme family, plays a critical role in the specific function of the sensor in determining the Phe. In one example, Omidinia and co-workers [206] immobilized enzyme of Phe-dehydrogenase on the n-propylamine functionalized mobile crystalline material 41 (MCM-41-nPrNH2). Then, it was casted to alter the surface of Au electrode. Under optimal conditions, Phe-dehydrogenase-MCM-41-nPrNH2/Au electrode exhibited a linear range of 0.01 to 0.15 μM and a LOD of 0.006 μM for the determination of Phe. In this method, the biosensor is prepared with a low-cost approach and has high accuracy in detection due to the presence of mesoporous materials (MCM-41) as suitable matrices for enzyme immobilization. Mesoporous materials with high thermal stability, large pore size, and uniformity are interesting. However, MCM-41 are less important than innovative types of mesoporous (dendritic fibrous nanosilica) due to their lower thermal stability, smaller pore volume, and larger size. So, application DFNS for this purpose (enzyme biosensing of Phe) may be led to suitable analytical results.

In a another study, Ermiş and coworkers [207] built an EC sensor based on molecular printing technique to determine l-Phe in real samples. Thiophen-3 carbonyl tryptophan (TP3C-Trp), which was generated as a monomer, was employed in this molecular imprinting strategy. They used Phe as the pattern molecule and engraved in attendance of TP3C-Trp and thiophen, used as functional monomers. Electropolymerization of thiophen was performed on an Au electrode and a sensor was prepared to measure the target (l-Phe). Using this sensor, linear range and LOD of Phe are 0.01 to 0.1 μM and 1 nM, respectively. In this method, the use of molecularly engraved polymer technology with specific binding sites of target molecules is naturally an advantage. As a result, it has increased sensitivity and selectivity of detection system. Interestingly, Hu et al., [208] created a sensor based on polyaniline (PAN) modified carbon electrode (CE) by combining β-cyclodextrin (β-CD)-MWCNTs composite materials and a sol–gel imprinted film. The developed sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP)/β-CD–MWCNTs/PAN/CE had a concentration range of 0.5 μM to 0.1 mM and a LOD of 1 nM. Because of the presence of β-CD–MWCNTs composite film and PAN layer, this method showed fantastic properties such as high stability, excellent electrocatalytic ability, and superior conductivity. However, it should also be noted that MWCNTs have potential toxicity.

In 2017, Zaidi and collegues [209] developed a protocol for detecting Phe enantiomers. This protocol includes an EC sensor. In this method, GCE modified by β-CD/rGO and used for the identification of Phe in real samples. The sensor showed a linear range of 0.4 to 40 μM and a LOD of 0.15 μM to d-Phe. For d-Phe, this sensor had a LOD of 0.1 μM. Similar concentration range was obtained for both enantiomers. Stabilization of β-CD on graphene sheets, because of the elevated conductance of graphene plates and large surface area, has led to the development of an excellent system for measuring the enantiomers of the target molecule (Phe).

Interestingly, Naghib and coworkers [210] prepared a hybrid nanocomposite of graphene oxide nanosheets (GO)-chitosan film. Then, in the last step of preparation the Phe dehydrogenase (PDH) was adsorbed on a layer of GO-chitosan film (Fig. 5 ). The prepared bio-sensor was analyzed and evaluated by CV and DPV techniques. It was found that this method has a linear range of 500 nM to 15 mM and a LOD of 416 nM for the L- Phe target. By mixing GO and polymer, a stable mixture is created and due to its high conductivity, it produces a small electrical penetration threshold. Chitosan is also suitable for creating enzymatic substrates by providing a viscous substrate and a compatible environment for enzymes and proteins.

All of the developed sensors and biosensor for detection Phe are listed in Table 8 and thire analytical performance were compared. The two systems in examples 2 and 3 together have better LODs than the other studies. In the study of omidinia reserch group, intelligently and simultaneously used MCM-41 along with the enzyme, which led to the emergence of a specific biosensor for the identification of Phe. However, we suggest using aptamer along with SiNPs such as DFNS to achieve better responses. This suggestion is because aptamer is more stable and specific function compared to enzyme and also DFNS has properties such as more stability, larger pore volume, less toxicity, and smaller size compared to MCM-41.

Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+) is a co-enzyme that is found in living cells and is used as a facilitator of electron transfer in reactions. In one example, Fazial and Tan [211] proposed a fluorescence biosensor to determine Phe. This method focuses on examining of the fish freshness using bio-fluids from the fish body. This sensor was prepared based on NAD+ and Phe dehydrogenase adsorbed on the graphene oxide-chitosan (GO/CT) layer. The GO/CT nanocomposites biofilm applied as transducer of biosensor. Using this enzymatic biosensor, Phe was determined in the linear range of 0.030 to 0.240 mM and a LOD of 0.020 mM. The combination of GO with CT resulted in a good nanocomposite for the development of the above enzyme sensor. In 2015, Mishra et al. [212] proposed a system to develop a fluorescence sensor for Phe identification. They have a new strategy called calix (resorcinarene tetra hydrazide (RTH)) for this purpose. RTH was used as a reducing and stabilizing agent to synthesize of AuNPs. RTH-AuNPs appeared successful in detecting the target by turning off the fluorescence signal. This method showed a linear range of 100 to 820 nM and LOD of 20 nM.

With its controlled morphology and size, AuNPs create unique optoelectronic properties in the field of biosensor technology. Recently, Sathya and coworkers [213] proposed a novel method for the Phe sensing using pyridine based organic fluorophore. Under optimal conditions of fluorophore, this sensor showed linear range 20 nM to 500 μM and LOD 0.32 nM. Because of their cost-effectiveness, the simplicity and wide detection range of fluorescent organic compounds are interesting in biomarkers recognition. Looking at the performance of the fluorescence sensors, it is clear that the Sathya platform has more efficient results. Finally, for the optical detection of Phe, we recommend using NPs such as AgNPs, PdNPs, ZrO2NPs, CuNPs, SnO2NPs, GQDs, DFNS and MCM-41. We predict an increase in the developing of micro-sensors and smart phone sensors.

The molecular imprinting procedure is a significant system with particular cavities for recognition of object molecules in a polymeric matrix. In 2021, for fast and sensitive measurement of l-Phe, Çimen et al., [214] engineered an SPR based sensor. The non-imprinted and imprinted poly (2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-methacryloyl-l- Phe) layers of l – Phe polymeric were synthesized at the SPR chip plane using ultraviolet polymerization. The above SPR sensor provided a LOD of 0.0085 μM in the linear range of 5 to 400 μM. Based on the experimental results, this sensor can be suggested in clinical applications for Phe monitoring. The lack of progress of optical sensors of the SPR type is quite clear. In this regard, we predict that researchers use biological agents such as DNA, aptamer, enzyme and gene as well as new nanomaterials such as silicate nanomaterials and carbon-based nanomaterials to address the existing gap.

In 2019, Beigi et al. [215] developed an ECL-based sensor for Phe detection. They modified GCE electrode with Ru(bpy)3 2+/Sm2O3NPs/chitosan composite. The prepared Sm2O3/Ru(bpy)3 2+/chitosan/GCE made a suitable signal after addition of the analyte. This method also showed a concentration range of 0.05 nM to 0.5 μM and a LOD of 0.014 nM. The presence of Sm2O3 NPs has been shown to increase the ECL signal. These NPs have recently been used in biochemical sensors. This could be a promising issue for the development of more sensors for a variety of analytes.

Table 8 summarizes the reports related to the measurement of Phe. Until now, all kinds of sensors have been functionalized with AuNPs, AgNPs, rGO, MWCNTs, MCM-41 and Sm2O3 NPs. Accordingly, we understand the high importance of nano-materials in this research area. It is also possible to use metallic nanoparticles such as PdNPs, ZrO2, CuNPs, SnO2NPs, and carbon-based nanomaterials such as GQDs, SWCNTs, and hybrid and silicate nanomaterials. On the other hand, the use of different types of aptamers, peptides and genes can be effective in creating more selective and sensitive sensors. For SPR and ECL methods, only one study was found, which needs to be developed. ECL method platform with LOD equal to 0.014 nM had the best response. Also, the EC, fluorescence, SPR, and ECL sensors had detection ranges of 1 to 416 nM, 0.32 to 20000 nM, 8.5 nM and 0.014 nM, respectively. For Phe, other methods such as FRET, PEC and piezoelectric systems have not been considered yet. Finally, it is predicted to develop micro and smart phone sensors for detection of Phe.

Tyrosine (Tyr) is an essential aromatic AA to maintain your body's nutritional balance [219]. Tyr is a derivative of Phe and is a major component of the protein in the body. It acts as a primary material for the physiological synthesis of neurotransmitters such as epinephrine, l-dopa, dopamine, and norepinephrine through a sequence of biological events [220]. Changes in the concentration of Tyr in the body is associated with depression, emotional disorders, and Parkinson disease [221]. l-Tyr is effective in relieving anxiety, headaches, and irritability [222]. It is worth noting that d -Tyr is an unnecessary AA and operates as an artificial block for drug preparation [223]. With these explanations, the importance of identifying this AA becomes more colorful. So, this section is focused on the different materials/methods for the sensing and biosensing of Tyr. Also, significant advantages and limitation of techniques are addressed towards finding of solution to improvement of detection strategies.

Hemin is a biological agent (porphyrin containing iron and chlorine) that effectively electrocatalysis the oxidation of the analyte. In a research in 2012, Wei et al., [224] proposed a biosensor based on hemin modified graphene nanosheet (HGN) which was constructed and used for the monitoring of Tyr. To produce this platform, researchers used a safe and green thermal method for rGO synthesis. The results obtained from different techniques confirmed that hemin was immobilized on the rGO nanoplate through π-π interaction. HGN/GCE was applied for the measurement of Tyr which LOD this method is 75 nM with dynamic range of 0.5 to 20 μM. High stability, easy manufacturing, easy control, and economic efficiency of electroactive biosensors make them valuable. According to the oxidation data, it is known that rGO increased the electron transfer between hemin and the electrode. In a study conducted by Yola et al., [225] a sensor designed based on cubic Au NPs (cAuNPs) trapped in 2-aminoethanethiol (2-AET) functionalized with GO.The prepared cAuNPs/2-AETGO/GCE for the determination of Tyr was approved according to the ICH guidelines and showed significant accuracy and sensitivity to Tyr in milk samples. This method has a linear range of 1 to 20 nM and a LOD of 0.15 nM. Recently, AuNPs have been used in various studies due to the properties such as high electron transfer rate, increased electrode conductivity, and increased analytical sensitivity. The simultaneous use of Au and GO NPs in this study made this sensor a suitable platform for the analysis of Tyr. Recently, Deng and coworkers [226] modified a small paste electrode (with an inner diameter of 0.8 mm) using combination of acetylene black (AB) and reduced GO. The modified electrode (rGO/ABPE (0.8 mm)) displayed a linear range for Tyr measurement between 0.1 and 100 μM and a LOD of 0.06 μM. In this method, due to the synergistic effect of AB and reduced GO, a significant oxidation peak current was obtained. Also, compared to normal-size electrodes, this small electrode has a faster response, less ohmic drop, increased current density, and improved signal-to-noise ratio. In another example, dong and colleagues [227] engineered a sensor by combining GQDs and β-CDs. In this work, they modified a GCE with a β-CDs-GQDs composite to sensitively determine Tyr enantiomers. The engineered system exhibited a concentration range of 6 to 100 μM and a LOD of 6.07 nM for l-Tyr. It also had a concentration range of 0.1 to 1 mM for d-Tyr and LOD of 0.103 μM. The combination of the properties of two materials that have recently been used by researchers in the design of sensors created a suitable sensor for measuring target enantiomers in the best possible way.

In another similar example, Karthika and co-workers [228] produced a new sensor based on copper oxide decorated with β-CD based on sonochemical synthesis. The CuO/β-CD was then distributed in the Nafion (Nf) and a homogeneous suspension solution was obtained. Drop-casting was used to stabilize the CuO/-CD/Nf suspension on GCE surface. After fully preparing the sensor, they proceeded to determine l-Tyr (Fig. 6 ). In the analytical stages, CuO/β-CD/Nf/GCE showed a concentration range of 0.01 to 100 μM and a LOD of 8.2 nM. The synthesis of CuO nanomaterials in this study is done by novel method that does not require a long synthesis time, complex synthesis steps, high pressure, and temperature.

In 2020, Roy et al., [229] used a templated over oxidized polypyrrole (PP) layer coated on ITO as a sensor. Analytical data from this sensor showed a linear range of 100 fM to 1 mM and a LOD of 0.0063 nM from EIS technique. This method has the advantage that it can be transferred to other substrates (electrodes) if the electron transfer kinetics of ferrocyanide occur faster. Unfortunately, this system has not been utilized yet in clinical applications. In another study, Gu and colleagues [230] used a low-cost method to detect Tyr. Using CuO/Cu2O/MWCNTs/GCE a linear range was from 0.2 μM to 0.2 mM with LOD of 9.6 nM in phosphate buffer solution. It is also a low-cost and sensitive design for targeting human urine using a nanocomposite. In another study, Murtada and others [231] modified a SPCE with aluminum-doped copper selenide NPs (Al-CuSeNPs) for l-Tyr analytical study. The above architecture showed a linear range of 0.15 to 10 μM and LOD of 0.04 μM in pharmaceutical samples. In the above work, Murtada's group has increased the efficiency of this system by trapping aluminum with CuSeNPs. Of course, the role of CuSeNPs is also colorful and has recently attracted attention in biomedical fields such as diagnosis and treatment. As can be seen, the sensor designed by the Roy group showed a better LOD than previous reports. To improve the EC sensors for the determination of Tyr, we suggest paying attention to the following points: I) Using nanomaterials such as carbon nanomaterials, metal NPs, silicate nanomaterials and hybrid nanomaterials. II) Using economic aptamers, DNA with specific function and peptides. III) The use of labeled sensors such as toluidine blue (TB) for quick and accurate identification. IV) Development of a biosensor with the ability to simultaneously measure several AA.

At a glance, the performance of all EC sensors for analytical comparison is provided in Table 9 . EC platforms in the determination of Tyr had the largest number, and special attention has been paid to different NPs (such as graphene, Au, rGO, GQDs, Cu and MWCNTs) in their development. In addition, the developed EC sensors investigated for Tyr had a detection range of 0.00173 to 103 nM. However, in the development of these sensors, aptamer, enzyme, gene, peptide and other biological factors have not been used yet, and bio-receptor has been used in only one case of research. Also, as a research gap, it should be mentioned that no research has been presented for optical, ECL, PEC and piezoelectric platforms. In the end, we hope that in the very near future, using the advantages of nanomaterials and biomaterials, stable, cheap, simple and portable microsensors can be developed to determine Tyr.

In the human body, one of the essential AAs that is important for most of the physiological functions is tryptophan (Trp). Trp affects mental health, sleep, and mood by acting as a precursor of melatonin and serotonin. It is also effective in maintaining nitrogen balance [232]. If not metabolized properly or consumed in large concentrations, it may lead to hallucinations and delusions [233]. In addition, Trp deficiency can cause Alzheimer's disease [234]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the minimum required amount of this essential AA is 4 mg per 1000 g of body weight [235]. Due to the fact that the body is not able to synthesize it directly, so the required amount must be provided through diet and medication sources. Therefore, due to its small threshold between toxic and essential levels, the importance of sensitive and accurate determination of Trp is clear [236]. In this section, application of EC and optical methods for the detection and determination of Trp in real samples are studied. Also, role of utilized nanomaterials in this case are discussed. Finally, advantages and limitation are surveyed.

Met is one of the AAs that is easily polymerized on the surface of the electrode with the CV technique, which has been used in various researches to detect and measure different analytes such as dopamine, uric acid, and food colors. Wang et al., [237] modified the GCE surface with poly-l-Met (PLME) and a graphene (GR) composite layer towards sensitive and selective detection of Trp in the presence of dopamine. Under optimal conditions, prepared sensor (PLME/GR/GCE) showed a linear range of 0.05 to 10 μM and LOD of 0.017 μM. Polymer film-modified substrates are of great interest due to their strong adhesion to the electrode surface, biocompatibility, homogeneity, and good stability. Also, polymer firm providing a suitable substrate for the stabilization of graphene layer, which itself increases the EC signal, has led to the successful operation of this sensor. Tian et al., [238] engineered chitosan as a polymer, sulfuric acid as a crosslinking agent, and Trp as a template to prepare an electrical conductive of MIP layer on the surface of an acetylene black paste electrode (ABPE). According to the obtained analytical results, the printed electrode has a linear range of 0.01 to 4 µM, 4 to 20 µM, 20 to100 µM, and LOD of 8.0 nM for determination of Trp. Chitosan due to biocompatibility, non-toxicity, low cost, high ability to form thin films, low cost, high chemical/physical stability, and excellent electrical properties fascinated the consideration of researchers. At the same time, the MIP based sensors yet offered some shortcomings such as slow rebinding/desorbing rate, low imprinting capacity/density, and high non-specific adsorption. Xia and colleagues [239] coated MIP of dual-function monomers and functional 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ionic liquid (IL) modified with MWCNTs (MWCNTs@IL) on the GCE. Finally, the Nafion solution was added to the MIP-MWCNTs@IL/GCE and the sensor was utilized for determination of Trp with a linear range of 8 nM to 26 µM and a LOD of 6 nM. The resulting assay introduced sensitivity, rebinding/desorbing rate, and excellent selectivity. In another example conducted by Peng and coworkers [234], metal–organic scaffold MIL-101(Fe) (MIL-101) decorated with AgNPs casted on GCE. Using this sensor the Trp was determined in a linear range of 1 to 50 µM and 50 to 150 µM with LOD of 0.14 µM. AgNPs/MIL-101 due to the combination of MIL-101 and AgNPs, has high electron transfer properties, suitable sensitivity, high available surface area, and chemical resistance on the recognition of Trp by this sensor. In 2020, Zhang and collegues [240] loaded cerium oxide (CeO2) NPs on RGO in an easy way, resulting in a CeO2/RGO nanocomposite. This EC platform had a linear detection range of 0.2 to 25 μM with LOD of 80 nM. CeO2 has been used as electrode material in the above study, but it does not have sufficient electrical conductivity and reduce the sensitivity. However, the presence of RGO along with this rare earth metal oxide has increased the electrocatalytic activity of this sensor. As a result, the creation of this nanocomposite has covered the limitation of neat CeO2. In another similar example, He and coworkers [241] prepared an EC sensor for detection of Trp using rGO (ERGO) and Cu2O toward low-cost analysis. With this nanocomposite (Cu2O–ERGO), they modified the surface of GCE. Analytical data exhibited that Cu2O–ERGO/GCE showed good electrocatalytic activity toward oxidation of Trp and under optimal conditions this sensor had a linear range of 0.02 to 20 μM with LOD of 0.01 μM. Recently, Cu2O NPs have been considered due to their good chemical stability, easy preparation and functionalization, abundance, significant catalytic activity, and low cost. In 2021, Sundaresan et al., [242] developed an EC sensor using manganese tungstate-nanoplates-encapsulated (MnWO4) modified rGO. MnWO4/rGO/GCE was tested for detection of Trp in milk samples by electro analysis techniques and it was found that this method has a linear range of 0.001 to 120 μM. It was also found to have a LOD of 4.4 nM. MnWO4 is one of the interesting metal oxide nanomaterials with properties such as low melting point, high sensitivity, excellent magnetic features, and high electrical conductivity. The combination of MnWO4 with rGO is a good candidate to measurement of Trp. Interestingly, Li and colleagues [243] synthesized Pd-Cu@Cu2O/N-rGO by chemical reduction and hydrothermal processes. They first used N precursors to obtain N-rGO sheets through a hydrothermal method. Using ascorbic acid and glucose, the Pd-Cu@Cu2O was placed on pre-prepared N-rGO plates. Finally, GCE was modified with Pd-Cu@Cu2O/N-rGO to detect Trp. The sensor developed under optimal conditions had a linear range of 0.01 to 40.0 µM and a LOD of 1.9 nM. The presence of N-rGO sheets and Pd-Cu@Cu2O particles increased the oxidation response and showed an excellent electrocatalytic performance and reduced the oxidation potential in this sensor.

The other sensors developed to determine Trp are summarized in Table 10 . As is evident from all EC studies, the Li group study has a lower LOD than the others. At the end of this section, we are willing to eagerly invite researchers to use new nanopolymers, carbon nanomaterials, new silicate nanomaterials, as well as magnetic NPs, along with biological agents such as DNA and aptamer, for the further development of Trp EC sensors.

Zhang et al. [244] used hydrothermal carbon spheres (HTCs) modified with 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid (3-APBA) to achieve a fluorescence sensor for detection of l-Trp with a linear range of 0.04 to 0.32 mM and a LOD of 0.005 mM. The above system can selectively change the on–off function. Economic and environmentally friendly HTCs are among carbon materials that are also used in sensor engineering. However, it has almost no ability to determine Trp by fluorescence assay system. Also, Tayade et al., [245] prepared disulfide-based, imine-linked fluorescent receptor for the detection of Trp DMSO/water with a linear range of 0 to 5 μM and a LOD of 47.6 nM. Non-covalent interactions between hydroxyl, sulfur, and imine functional groups have resulted in sensitive target selection in this method. In 2016, Wei and coworkers [246] designed a functionalized pillar [5] arene-based fluorescence assay to determine l-Tryp. The BTAP5-sensor showed LOD of 0.283 μM in DMSO/water solution. Functional pillararenes are considered on benzene rings due to the easier operation by different alternatives. Interestingly, Zhong et al. [247] used an europium-functionalized organometallic probe (Eu3+@UiO-66-FDC) for the optical recognition of Trp. Eu3+@UiO-66-FDC brought good sensitivity to the target analyte among other interfering factors in biological and food samples. Also, this platform with a linear range of 0 to 1000 μM showed a LOD of 0.29 μM. In addition, it could successfully determine the target in rabbit milk and blood serum. This system was reliable and recyclable, which is also responsive in food products and biological fluids. The proposed system by Tayade group [245] showed higher sensitivity than other methods. At the end of this section, we predict that researchers to use new carbon nanomaterials, nano-polymers, new silicate nanomaterials, as well as magnetic NPs, along with biological agents such as aptamer and DNA, for the further development of Trp fluorescence sensors. Also, the development of portable and cheap chips that can determine several AA at the same time is predictable.

All the results related to Trp detection are summarized in Table 10. The presence of metal NPs (Ag, Cu2O and AuNPs) and carbon-based nanomaterials (rGO and MWCNTs) alone or by creating new composites has increased the sensitivity of sensors. However, NPs have not been used much in the development of fluorescence probes. Therefore, it is suggested to use silicate based nanomaterials, hybrid nanomaterials, new nanocomposites, carbon-based nanomaterials, and metal NPs in the development of fluorescence probes for Trp. On the other hand, there is a lack in the utilizing of biological factors in the development of Trp detection platforms; therefore, it is expected to see an increase in the application of aptamer, peptide, enzyme, and gene to provide the sensors with specific performances. In the case of Trp, more attention has been paid to EC platforms and these systems also had better analytical data. Optical systems have received less attention than EC, and can be explored further in this area. In addition, the developed EC and fluorescence sensors investigated for Trp had a detection range of 1.9 to 390 nM and 47.6 to 5000 nM. Finally, we did not find specific research for the monitoring of Trp by ECL, PEC, piezoelectric methods. It is also recommended to use microchips. Biochip and microchips technology, with the minimum amount of samples, are suitable for outpatient analysis of patient samples by less skilled personnel, and eliminates the need for medical diagnostic laboratories. Also, it provides the possibility of analysis of multiple analytes in one sample at the same time and as a result provides the possibility of accurate and fast detection.

Proline (Pro) is synthesized from other AAs, especially GA. Pro is known as an unnecessary AA that has a secondary amino group in the alpha situation relative to the carboxyl group. Its high condensation in collagen is important as an element that plays a supporting role in bones, skin, and tendons. This AA is very important because of its widespread use in human life and especially in the synthesis of proteins in the body [251,252]. In this regard, its identification is also important. This part of review is focused on the survey of different method for the sensing Pro in different biological media. Also, utilization of nanostructure materials on the improvement of sensors performance are investigated. Finally, comparison of method and materials on the analytical efficiency of method are discussed.

Poly (Cys) as a new biocomposite has attracted a lot of attention in the detection of different analytes such as adenine, guanine, ascorbic acid, and etc. In 2018, Hasanzadeh and coworkers [253] successfully developed a system for the sensing of L- Pro in real samples. They prepared Au NPs (GNPs) using the soft pattern method. The prepared NPs were then stabilized on a biocompatible nanocomposite containing poly-l-Cys as a conductive matrix and GQDs as a signal amplifier. Then, a new multilayer film was generated on the surface of GCE. The poly-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-GCE showed a linear range of 0.5 nM to 10 mM with low limit of quantification (LLOQ) of 0.1 nM. The presence of two unique NPs, GQDs and GNPs, has resulted in a highly sensitive sensor. Also, Hasanzadeh and colleagues [254] trapped Pro dehydrogenase (PRODH) enzyme in magnetic mesoporous silica nanomaterials (Fe3O4-MCM-41-nPrNH2). Trapped PRODH enzyme was stabilized on poly-Cys supported CS modified GCE. A biosensor designed (PRODH-Fe2O3-MCM-41-nPrNH2-PCys-CS-GCE) used to determine l-Pro in phosphate buffer solution in linear range of concentration 0.01 to 0.15 µM with LOD of 0.006 µM. High capacity of silica mesoporous NPs for the trapping of PRODH is a significant advantage of this biosensor. This approach has high stability. Together with the NPs of the other materials, they all worked together to create a successful strategy. The first study [253] is prioritized because of the lower LOD. Considering the presence of biosensor, it is suggested to develop other biosensors in this field using aptamer, gene, peptide, and newly synthesized enzymes. We also predict that researchers design simple and precise nanochips using magnetic nanomaterials and carbon-based nanomaterials.

Using ECL technique, Hosseini et al. [255] engineered an interface for quantifying l -Pro based on the production of an ECL signal by a reaction between l-Pro and Ru(bpy)3 2+ on a carbon paste electrode (CPE) modified by samarium oxide (Sm2O3) NPs. Under experimental conditions, Sm2O3NPs-Ru(bpy)3 2+-CPE has a concentration range of 1 nM to 0.165 μM and a LOD of 0.6 nM. The use of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in ECL systems is closely followed. CPE, on the other hand, is valuable because of its easy preparation for determining target molecules. Among the various NPs, Sm2O3NPs are promising candidates that also performed satisfactorily.Interestingly, Wang et al. [256] proposed a new design to determine Pro enantiomers. In this design, they modified GCE with β-CD-reduced graphene oxide (β-CD-rGO) and Ru(bpy)3 2+-AuNPs (Ru-AuNPs). This platform was found that to be sensitive for diagnosis of d-Pro. This sensor had a LOD of 25 and 33 μM for d-Pro and l-Pro, respectively, in the linear range of 0.1 to 5 mM. The presence of β-CD-rGO improved the selectivity of this sensor. Among the two existing methods, the first technique showed better LOD. It should be mentioned that enzyme was used to determine Pro, however, it is predicted that various biological agents will be used in the development of ECL-based biosensors. In addition, the use of new nanomaterials and new luminols are suggested to increase the sensitivity of the ECL based sensors.

Considering the results in Table 11 , few EC sensors for determining Pro are reported till now. Also, there are only two studies for measuring the target molecule with ECL technique, which did not show good analytical results compared to EC methods. EC and ECL based sensors have a detection range of 0.1 to 6 nM and 0.6 to 33000 nM for Pro. In all researches, the advantages of various types of NPs (Au, Sm2O3 NPs, MCM-41 and GQDs) have been used, which have increased the accuracy of the platforms. On the other hand, in EC platforms, biological agents (poly-Cys and PRODH) have been used to create specific and sensitive functions. Aptamers, new enzymes, genes, cells and antibodies can also be used. On the other hand, there is no optical sensor to determine Pro. Also, no PEC and piezoelectric sensors were found to measure the target molecule. In addition, it is expected that the number of optical, PEC, piezoelectric and even EC and ECL sensors will increase in the near future. On the other hand, it is expected that nanomaterials, especially new mesoporous silica NPs, will be used more in the development of these systems, because they create a very suitable approach as high-level materials. In the end, we hope to develop portable, simple, cheap and stable chips using biotechnology and nanotechnology, which can have a very specific function due to the presence of biological agents.

The simplest and lightest AA of all living cells is glycine (Gly). In the human body, it acts as a suppressor of neurotransmitter in the brain and spinal cord [257]. Gly plays a neuroprotective role in anoxia, hypoxia, and ischemia–reperfusion. Deficiency of Gly can lead to stiff baby syndrome, seizures, slow growth, startle disease, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Because it plays a significant role in human health, its measuring and diagnosis is a vital task [258–262]. So, in this part of article, we critically discussed significance of different materials/methods on the sensitive recognition of Gly in biofluids. Importantly, some of research gaps in this area are discovered. Also, new approach for each type of sensing method is proposed.

Glycine oxidase (GlyOx) is an enzyme that is effective in catalysis and creating a specific function in analyte identification. Recently, Wang and coworkers [263] proposed a biosensor for detection of Gly in biological fluids. They designed this platform through a novel quinoprotein with GlyOx activity and the specific ability to oxidize Gly. To use the quinoprotein, they encapsulated specific enzyme of Gly inside the chitosan matrix by adding a layer of Nafion. Under optimal conditions, quinoprotein with GlyOx activity in the concentration range of 25 to 500 μM with LOD of 11.1 μM. Easy preparation and acceptable analytical properties make this approach valuable. In one example, Alam and colleagues [264] successfully designed a biosensor for Gly recognition. For fabrication of this sensor, a triple ZnO/Al2O3/Cr2O3 NPs with a heat dissolution strategy was prepared and then a layer of triple NPs prepared on the surface GCE by aiding Nafion as adhesive agent. The developed platform has a concentration range of 0.1 nM to 1.0 μM with LOD of 82.25 ± 4.11 pM toward Gly detection. Recently, semiconductors based on metal oxides have been used in the development of sensors. ZnO with suitable stability and good electronic properties as well as Al2O3 with a strong conductivity and finally Cr2O3 with interesting chemical, optical, and electrical properties are present in the above strategy as a representative of transition metals toward increment of sensor performance. In another EC design, Azadbakht and Abbasi [265] prepared a Gly sensor by electro-deposition of nickel chelidamic acid (Ni–CHE) complex on the Au–PtNPs/ nanofibers (NFs)/GCE. The engineered interface (Ni–CHE/AuNPs/NFs/GCE) showed a linear range and a LOD of 1 μM to 0.75 mM and 0.3 μM, respectively for the determination of Gly in real samples. It is important to point out that, these composite exhibits properties such as good flexibility, suitable lightness, excellent thermal and chemical stability. The use of NPs with their amazing properties facilitates electron transfer, which is important in sensor development. It should be noted that among the existing EC methods, the design of Alam’s group has improved LOD effectively. This reflects the fact that their strategy has been more sensitive in sensing of Gly. Despite the importance of Gly detection, a few studies have been reported in this regard. MIP and zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF) are known as new polymers that have good chemical stability, high specific surface area, and convenient synthesis. Combination of these polymers with nanoparticles with high electrical conductivity such as Au, Ag and PtNPs provide hybrid materials for detection of Gly. On the other hand, due to the use of enzyme in one study, it is predicted that aptamer, cell, gene and peptide will also be used in future studies.

In general, there is a lack of various types of biosensors for determination of Gly in real samples. On the other hand, only one enzyme GlyOx [263]) is used in Gly detection which, however, did not show high analytical result. As can be seen in Table 12 , in two studies, a more appropriate result has been obtained with the presence of NPs (ZnO, Al2O3, Cr2O3, and Au). Also, EC approaches for detection of Gly are not well developed and there are few studies. The developed EC sensors had a detection range of 0.08225 to 11100 nM. Importantly, there is no optical sensor for measuring Gly, which is expected to be developed and improved in these types of platforms. On the other hand, there is no study for ECL, PEC and piezoelectric based systems for the Gly recognition. Therefore, the use of MIP and ZIF is also expected for the development of these sensors. Finally, it is recommended to use the advantages of NPs such as Au, Ag, Cu, Pt, carbon-based NPs, dendric fibrose silicate NPs, graphene oxides and etc. for the architecture of sensitive sensors. In addition, considering that the enzyme has been used in the EC detection of Gly, it is recommended to use aptamer, enzyme, gene, cell and peptide in the development of optical, ECL and PEC sensors.

Alanine (Ala) is an essential AA. Chiral AAs play an important role in the metabolism of living organisms [266,267]. The high importance of determining of L-AA has been proven to assess the quality of medicine, beverages, and food. On the other hand, D AA is associated with some problems such as stomach cancer, epilepsy, Alzheimer, and schizophrenia [268,269]. In the meantime, Ala is also a chiral AA, so its identification is important for human health. Therefore, all of sensors developed for Ala are reviewed in this section. In addition, their analytical performance and role of detection techniques a long with type of utilized materials in this case are investigated for the discovery of efficient methods for clinical studies.

A specific flavoenzyme that can efficiently catalyze the production of ammonium and H2O2 from D-AA and contains flavine adenine dinucleotide is known as D-AA oxidase (DAAO) enzyme. In a study, Xia et al., [270] engineered a biosensor for Ala enantiomers based on 3,4,9,10-perylene tetracarboxylic acid-functionalized MWCNTs (PTCA-MWCNTs) and DAAO. In the final stage of preparation, the prepared sensor was successfully developed to determine the target molecules (d-Ala and l-Ala) and the receptors were placed on the GCE surface. The preparation steps are shown in Fig. 7 . DAAO/PTCA/MWCNTs/GCE used for D/l-Ala analysis which showed that it has a larger current response than type d-enantiomer. To d-Ala, this system showed a linear concentration range and a LOD of 10 nM to 1 mM and 3.3 nM, respectively. The presence of DAAO in this platform has made it specific for d-Ala identification. Along with DAAO, MWCNTs have been used due to the good conductivity of these nanomaterials. However, because these carbon nanomaterials are poorly dispersed in water, PTCA has been used to improve their dispersibility.

Till now, an EC sensor, Liu et al., [271] used Fe3O4@Au@Ag@CuxO NPs for sensing of d-Ala in real samples. The above platform had a concentration range of 100 pM to 10 μM and a LOD of 52 pM. NPs with a core–shell structure can amplify the signal by varying the type and density of the shells, and by shortening the electron emission distance, it is easier to regulate electrical signals. The controllable integration of NPs used in this platform has enhanced the accuracy of the sensor. In 2014, Dai et al., [272] used an electrodeposition method for fabrication of nano-porous nickel (NP-Ni) coating on the surface of a boron-doped diamond (BDD) electrode. This is a suitable strategy for the EC recognition of l-Ala. The NP-Ni/BDD film showed a concentration range of 0.5 to 4.5 μM with LOD of 0.01 μM. In this research work [272], BDD, one of the promising substrates for electro-analytical purpose, was used to construction of sensor. Low voltammetry background of currents, high corrosion resistance, biocompatibility, ineffective surface, and a wide potential window are its features. The presence of NP-Ni on the surface of this electrode is the key to a good design. However, the above study did not show good sensitivity. It is important to point out that this type of electrode is less used by researchers in previous reports which is probably due to their difficult preparation and relatively high cost.

In a similar study, Shoja and co-workers [273] modified GCE using MWCNTs and Au nanofilms (Au-NF) and then stabilized DAAO on its surface. It turned out that Au-NF/MWCNT/GCE has a linear range of 0.25 to 4.5 μM with LOD of 20 nM for Ala diagnosis under optimal conditions. This approach can be introduced as a suitable candidate for sensor development due to the use of nanocomplex. However, the accumulation of MWCNTs is not well controlled and this can be considered limiting. As it turns out, the strategy of Liu et al., with a LOD of 52 pM is a worthwhile approach. The lack of reports based on MIP and ZIF polymers can be seen. In addition to enzymes, we also recommend the use of aptamers, DNA and cells. Sensors will likely become small, stable, inexpensive, and simple in the near future, so that even without the need for a professional operator, they can monitor the body's health.

In the interesting research work, Lu and coworkers [274] proposed a new optical platform for the sensitive determination of Ala. The system is based on the utilization of manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets and poly [9,9-bis (6-N, N, N trimethylammonium) hexyl] fluorenylene phenylene (PFP). With the presence of DAAO and the target analyte (d-Ala), enzymatic oxidase produces H2O2, which restores PFP fluorescence and disintegrates MnO2 nanoplates. MnO2 quenched PFP in the linear range of 1 nM to 1 mM with LOD of 0.35 nM. Along with PFP, MnO2 nanoplates have made an impact in this platform by providing good light stability, excellent solubility in water and high electron transfer.

In 2016, Singh and Buragohain [275], using the SPR property of AgNPs, engineered a novel optical probe to detect d-Ala. Simultaneously with the reaction of d-Ala with DAAO, surface plasmon of AgNPs decreases due to H2O2 production, which can be justified by spectrophotometric technique. This system showed a linear range of 0.1 to 5 mM and a LOD of 1 mM for the measurement of Ala in plasma-like liquid.

In another study, Lin et al. [277] created a stereo-selective biosensor based on amplified ECL signal of luminol with rGO-hemin (H)-Cys composite, Au–platinum nanowires (Au-PtNWs), and DAAO. They modified the GCE with the above-mentioned materials along with nanowires. This design showed higher intensity for d-Ala than l-Ala, which had a concentration range of 1 nM to 5 mM and a LOD of 0.33 nM. The rGO-(H)-Cys composite can amplify the signal alongside the Au-PtNWs, so the above design is a promising platform for the sensing of Ala. Among the studied methods, the second method has a more acceptable analytical performance than the first study.

The results of the different sensors used to measure Ala are summarized in Table 13 . By comparing different sensors, it is clear that there is lack of aptamer, peptide and gen-based sensors for Ala determination. On the other hand, it is clear that researchers have paid special attention to NPs in the development of these types of sensors. They have used MWCNTs, Fe3O4@Au@Ag@CuxO NPs, NP-Ni, Au-NF, MnO2 nanosheets, AgNPs, Au-PtNPs, Au-PtNPs, Au-PtNW, and rGO to develop more sensitive platforms that have increased the sensitivity of sensing systems. Using NPs, EC, fluorescence, SPR and ECL sensors have detection ranges of 0.052 to 20 nM, 0.35 nM and 0.33 to 1.7 nM, respectively. It is also recommended to use other metallic NPs such as ZrO2 NPs, PdNPs and SeNPs, silicate nanomaterials and hybrid nanomaterials. It should be noted that only one fluorescence and SPR sensor are reported for the determination of Ala between optical methods, and the SPR system was less sensitive. Also, there are no PEC, FRET and piezoelectric sensors for the monitoring of Ala. Therefore, the need for more efforts to make new and sensitive sensors is expected. It is recommended to use the unique properties of different nanomaterials in their development. Finally, in order to increase productivity for a simple and better determination of Ala, it is expected that microchips and simple substrates such as ZIF and MIP will receive more attention.

Leucine (Leu) isone of the essentialandvitalAAsin humanbodybecause itaids the cell metabolism. Its major roles ae including adipose tissue homeostasis, blood glucose homeostasis, and hemoglobin production [278,279]. Hence its sensitive measurement is important for clinicians. In this section, applied sensors for detection of Leu are surveyed. Also, their advantages and limitation are investigated toward discovery of efficient sensors and biosensor for this purpose. Finally, some of research/technological gapes in this research field are highlighted.

Using EC method, a sensor for l-lucine (Leu) was developed by Hussain and colleagues [280]. They first prepared the strontium oxide nanorods (SrO NR) and then modified the GCE with a thin layer of these nanorods. Their system showed a linear range of 0.1 nM to 0.1 mM and a LOD of 37.5 ± 0.2 pM for l-Leu. The SrO NR used in this study create an electrocatalytic ability to measure l-Leu in real samples. In another example, Rezaei and Zare [281] designed a method to determine leucine in the urine and blood sample. To achieve this, they modified GCE with MWCNTs. The prepared sensor displayed a concentration range of 9 μM to 1.5 mM and LOD of 3 μM. This strategy is a simple method, but shows poor sensitivity. Among the sensing platforms examined by researcher, Musarraf Hussain's method showed better efficiency in defining Leu, and it can be referred to its lower LOD. Owing to the necessity of examining and sensing this essential AA in the body, extensive research was expected, but unfortunately, few studies have been done.

We summarized the available sensors for Leu measurement in Table 14 . First, it should be noted that immunosensors, aptasensors, gene-based sensors, and peptides were not used in the determination of Leu, and this is a clear research gap. On the other hand, only two EC studies were found for Leu detection, which needs special attention for the development of these sensors. These existing sensors had a concentration range of 0.0375 to 3200 nM. In the course of this development, application of various NPs can be used effectively. Moreover, we did not find any fluorescence, SPR, FRET, ECL, PEC and piezoelectric sensors for Leu detection. We hope that this gap will be resolved in the near future. It is expected that sensitive sensors with specific performance in the field of Leu detection will be designed using various types of NPs/nanomaterials along with various biological agents.

In summary, various types of sensors and biosensors as significant tools developed for the rapid, accurate and reliable recognition of AAs were reviewed. Our investigation showed that, various type of EC, optical, ECL, and PEC sensors/biosensor have been utilized to recognition of AAs. The most common type of sensor examined was the EC sensor. Fluorescence sensors have taken second place to determine most AAs after EC sensors. It should be noted that there are not many studies on identification of some AAs such as asparagine, glutamine, guanine, valine, serine, threonine, and isoleucine. NPs have been used to develop a variety of sensors. NPs endow different properties in sensors including: I) the intensive properties of NPs create a suitable substrate for interaction with analyte. II) Their electronic transmission improves the EC signal. III) Their small size compared to their bulky state gives them amazing properties. IV) They have significant optical properties that are excellent in optical sensors. V) They have high stability, which also increases the stability of the sensor. VI) They are economical in terms of cost. VII) They have special mechanical and thermal properties. VIII) With their electrocatalytic properties, they improve the performance of sensors. IX) Finally, they increase the sensitivity and selectivity of sensors in determining AA. We hope that more attention will be paid to nanotechnology in the development of new sensors to maximize the accuracy of these detection tools. On the other hand, different bioreceptors based on aptamers, enzymes, peptides and genes played an important role in creating specific and sensitive functions of some sensors, but unfortunately, these biological factors were not in most of the current researches. Among other biological agents, aptamers are more recommended for identifying AAs. In fact, aptasensors have many features such as: II) Thermal stability and high selectivity. III) Simple chemical modification capability with various labels such as fluorescence markers, EC, NPs and enzymes. IV) To be economical. V) Specific and repeatable results. In general, the methods that have simultaneously used biological agents and nanomaterials in their structure showed better analytical results compared to other examples. Also, if we pay attention to the analytical data for most of the AAs, acceptable results have been obtained, however, better results can be achieved using new nanomaterials along with biological agents. In addition, it is expected that shortcomings in determining of some AAs will be addressed by in future studies. Finally, we hope that sensors will be used as low-cost and sensitive tools soon as on-site and simple tools for bioanalysis approaches, especially the measurement of AAs. We enthusiastically propose the use of micro-biosensors and smart phone sensors for simple, inexpensive and operator-free analysis. We also suggest the using of new nanomaterials like silicate nanoparticles and also MIP, ZIF, MOFs and layered double hydroxide (LDH) which have recently gained more attention, to design chips for detection of several AAs at the same time. Finally, for reliable monitoring of AAs in human biofluids, it is suggested to develop on-site bio(sensors), utilization of smartphone techniques and chips that are capable of simultaneously/multiplex screening of AAs by using nanomaterials/different biological agents.
